column3: Solomon sat upon the throne of the Lord as king in place of his father David, and all were pleased and all Israel obeyed him. This signifies that Solomon's throne is called the throne of the Lord because he ruled as God's vicar and by divine appointment.
All the princes, mighty men, and all the sons of King David gave their hand in submission and were subject to King Solomon, signifying universal acceptance of his reign.
The Lord magnified Solomon above all Israel and gave him a glory of kingship such as no king of Israel before him had possessed, showing divine favor as the source of his greatness.
David reigned over all Israel for forty years: seven years in Hebron and thirty-three years in Jerusalem.
David died in a good old age, full of days, riches, and glory, and Solomon his son reigned in his place — a most blessed end to a most blessed reign.

The acts of King David, first and last, are written in the Book of Samuel the Seer, in the Book of Nathan the Prophet, and in the volume of Gad the Seer — three distinct prophetic records attesting to the fullness of David's history.
All the events of his kingdom, his might, and the times that passed under him, whether in Israel or in all the kingdoms of the lands, are comprehensively recorded in these prophetic writings.

The bronze altar refers to the altar of burnt offerings, on which victims were burned, and which was therefore made of bronze; for the other altar of incense, upon which incense was offered to God, was of gold.
He inquired — that is, by going to Gibeon, where the bronze altar was, so that he might offer a thousand victims upon it.

Solomon decreed to build a house for the name of the Lord, and a palace for himself. The chapter synopsis notes that Solomon designated 70,000 porters and 80,000 stonecutters, and made a pact with Hiram concerning a master architect and the sending of timber.
Solomon numbered seventy thousand men as burden-bearers on their shoulders, and eighty thousand to hew stones in the mountains, with three thousand six hundred overseers set over them.

Solomon sent to Hiram king of Tyre, recalling the agreement Hiram had made with David his father, by which cedar timber was sent so that David might build himself a house in which he also dwelt. This precedent forms the basis of Solomon's request.

Solomon declares the purposes of the Temple he intends to build: to burn incense before the Lord, to offer fragrant spices, to set forth the perpetual showbread, and to offer holocausts morning and evening, on sabbaths, new moons, and the solemn feasts of the Lord God of Israel — all things commanded to Israel in perpetuity.

The house which I desire to build is great, for our God is great above all gods. Solomon acknowledges the magnificence befitting God's house, grounding his ambitious building project in the surpassing greatness of the God to be worshipped there.

Solomon humbly confesses that no house can truly contain God: "If heaven and the heaven of heavens cannot contain Him, who am I that I should build Him a house?" The Temple is built not as God's dwelling per se, but only that incense might be burned before Him — an expression of profound theological humility. # GEM

Solomon requests from Hiram a skilled craftsman expert in gold, silver, bronze, iron, purple, crimson, and violet, and skilled in engraving — to work alongside the artisans whom David had already prepared in Judah and Jerusalem.
Solomon also requests cedar, cypress, and pine timber from Lebanon, knowing that Hiram's servants are skilled in felling timber there, with Solomon's own servants working alongside them.
The timber needed is great in quantity, for the house Solomon desires to build is exceedingly great and magnificent ("magna nimis et inclyta").

As payment for the woodcutters, Solomon offers twenty thousand cors of wheat, twenty thousand cors of barley, twenty thousand measures of wine, and twenty thousand measures of oil — provisions given as wages to Hiram's servants.
Hiram king of Tyre replied by letter to Solomon: "Because the Lord loves His people, He has made you king over them." Hiram recognizes divine providence in Solomon's elevation to kingship.

Hiram adds a blessing: "Blessed be the Lord God of Israel, who made heaven and earth, who gave to King David a wise, learned, and prudent son to build a house for the Lord and a palace for himself." Hiram, a Gentile king, here praises the God of Israel — a notable confession from one outside the covenant people.
Hiram declares he is sending Solomon a skilled and most learned man named Hiram, whom he calls "my father" — a title of honor and dignity indicating his pre-eminence among craftsmen.

This Hiram is described as the son of a woman of the daughters of Dan, whose father was a Tyrian. He is skilled in gold, silver, bronze, iron, marble, wood, purple, violet, fine linen, and crimson; he knows how to engrave every kind of engraving and to devise prudently whatever is necessary in any work — to collaborate with Solomon's artisans and those of David his father.
Hiram requests that Solomon send the wheat, barley, oil, and wine he had promised, as payment for the laborers.
Hiram promises that his servants will cut whatever timber is needed from Lebanon and float it in rafts by sea to Joppa, from where Solomon's men would transport it to Jerusalem.
Solomon numbered all the resident aliens (proselytes) in the land of Israel, after the census David his father had taken, and found one hundred fifty-three thousand six hundred.

From these Solomon made seventy thousand to carry burdens on their shoulders, eighty thousand to hew stones in the mountains, and three thousand six hundred overseers of the people's work — corresponding to the numbers given at the beginning of the chapter, thus forming a literary inclusio.
Lapide notes the enumeration of seventy thousand burden-bearers, citing this as evidence of the immense labor force Solomon conscripted for the construction of the Temple.

ON MOUNT MORIAH: on which Abraham once offered his son Isaac to God (Gen. 22:14). Hence on that same mount Christ, the antitype, offered himself as a holocaust to God for the salvation of the world. For Mount Calvary is a part or appendage of Mount Moriah.

THE LENGTH IN CUBITS ACCORDING TO THE FIRST MEASURE: because there was a second measure by which these 60 cubits were divided and apportioned into two sections, namely so that the Holy Place would have 40 cubits and the Holy of Holies the remaining 20. See what is said in 3 Kings ch. 6 v. 1 toward the end.
THE LARGER HOUSE (so he calls the Holy Place, which was larger than the Holy of Holies) HE COVERED WITH FIR PLANKS: made of fir, but with cedar ceiling-work intermixed, as is said in 3 Kings 6:9.

THE GOLD WAS MOST PURE: Hebrew reads "gold of Parvaim," that is, gold dug from the place called Ophir, and by aphaeresis Parvaim, or as the Septuagint renders it: gold from Pharuaim. So also Pagninus, Vatablus, Arias, Marinus, and others who think Parvaim is Peru. For the word and letters appear to be the same in both cases. Parvaim is said to be in the dual number because the region of Peru is twofold, namely greater and lesser. However, the name Peru was then unknown, and even now among the natives Pera is unknown. For that name was recently given to this region by the Spaniards, from a nearby river so called, as Joseph Acosta testifies, who lived in Peru for fifteen years (De Novo Orbe, bk. 1).

HE MADE THE HOUSE OF THE HOLY OF HOLIES: its length equal to the width of the house, twenty cubits, and its width likewise twenty cubits, and he covered it with gold plates amounting to about six hundred talents.

BEFORE THE DOORS OF THE TEMPLE TWO COLUMNS WHICH HAD THIRTY-FIVE CUBITS IN HEIGHT: Lapide notes this chapter is fully explained in his commentary on 3 Kings ch. 6, to which the reader is referred for detailed treatment of the temple's construction, the cherubim, the veil, and the two pillars Jachin and Boaz.

HE CALLED THE ONE ON THE RIGHT JACHIN, AND THE ONE ON THE LEFT BOAZ: the two pillars placed in the vestibule of the temple, one on the right and one on the left. This entire chapter has been explained at 3 Kings 6.
Solomon made the bronze altar twenty cubits long, twenty cubits wide, and ten cubits high, for burnt offerings.

The "line of thirty cubits" refers to the circumference or periphery of the molten sea, which was 30 cubits — that is, triple the diameter, as is geometrically consistent with a circular vessel ten cubits in diameter.

Beyond the twelve bronze oxen upon which the molten sea rested, there were on the front part of the sea, over a space of ten cubits (which was a third of the full circumference of thirty cubits), additional ox-heads in relief for ornamental purposes, and perhaps also to serve as spouts through which water could be drawn off. These heads were arranged in two rows (Hebrew: two orders), one row beneath the rim and another toward the base. They were cast together with the sea itself in the original casting, not affixed afterward from outside.

The molten sea rested upon twelve oxen: three facing north, three facing west, three facing south, and three facing east, with the sea placed upon them and their hindparts turned inward beneath the sea.
The thickness of the sea had the measure of a handbreadth, and its rim was like the rim of a cup or of an open lily; it held three thousand baths.
Solomon made ten lavers, placing five on the right and five on the left, for washing the things offered in burnt sacrifice; the priests themselves washed in the great sea.
He made ten golden lampstands according to the prescribed pattern and placed them in the temple, five on the right and five on the left.
He also made ten tables, placing them in the temple five on the right and five on the left, along with one hundred golden basins.

Solomon made the court of the priests and the great hall (basilica); according to the Hebrew and Septuagint, this was the great outer court where the people prayed — an open area enclosed on all sides by porticoes so that the people could take shelter there during rain. These porticoes were arranged in a triple colonnade with triple stories. This court of the people surrounded and enclosed the inner court of the priests.
The molten sea was placed on the right side, toward the east, in the direction of the south.
Hiram made the pots, the flesh-hooks, and the basins, completing all the work for King Solomon in the house of God.
This included the two pillars, the capitals, the chapiters, and the network coverings that covered the chapiters upon the pillars.
Also four hundred pomegranates and two networks, with two rows of pomegranates for each network, to cover the two bowls of the chapiters on top of the pillars.
He also made the bases and the lavers set upon the bases.
Hiram made for Solomon all these vessels for the house of the Lord out of the finest bronze — Hiram, who is here called Solomon's father (i.e., master craftsman and patron artisan).
The king cast them in the clay ground of the Jordan valley, between Succoth and Zeredathah.
The vessels were so numerous that the weight of the bronze could not be determined.
Solomon made all the vessels of the house of God: the golden altar, the tables with the shewbread upon them.
The lampstands with their lamps, to burn before the oracle according to the prescribed rite, all of purest gold.
The flowers, the lamps, and the tongs — all of purest gold.

The snuffers, the basins, the spoons, and the censers, all of purest gold. The doors of the inner temple (that is, of the Holy of Holies) and the outer doors of the temple were overlaid with gold. And so all the work that Solomon did for the house of the Lord was completed. The entire chapter is expounded in 3 Kings [1 Kings] chapter 7.

Solomon brought in all the things which David his father had vowed — silver, gold, and all the vessels — and placed them in the treasuries of the house of God. This fulfillment of David's votive offerings signifies that what parents dedicate to God, children ought to complete.

After this Solomon assembled the elders of Israel and all the princes of the tribes and the heads of families to bring the Ark of the Covenant of the Lord from the City of David, which is Sion. The Ark had rested in the tabernacle on Sion since David brought it there from the house of Obededom.

All the men of Israel came to the king on the solemn day of the seventh month — that is, on the feast of Tabernacles, which was the most solemn of all feasts, lasting eight days, beginning on the fifteenth of the seventh month (Tishri), so that the dedication of the Temple coincided with this great feast.

When all the elders of Israel had come, the Levites carried the Ark. It was the Levites' proper office to carry the Ark, as prescribed in the Law (Num. 4), though in 1 Kings 8:3 the priests are also mentioned as bearing it, since priests are a higher order of Levites.

They brought in the Ark and all the furnishings of the tabernacle; the vessels of the sanctuary that were in the tabernacle were carried by the priests together with the Levites. Thus the old tabernacle of Moses was now superseded by the permanent Temple, its vessels being transferred and incorporated into the new and more glorious house of God.

King Solomon and the whole assembly of Israel who were gathered before the Ark sacrificed sheep and oxen without number, so great was the multitude of victims. This immense sacrifice prefigures the one Sacrifice of Christ, which is of infinite value and offered for all peoples.

The priests brought the Ark of the Covenant of the Lord to its place, that is, to the inner sanctuary of the Temple — the Holy of Holies — beneath the wings of the cherubim. The Holy of Holies, being the innermost and most sacred space, represents heaven itself, into which Christ as High Priest entered with His own blood (Heb. 9:12).

The cherubim spread their wings over the place where the Ark was set, and covered the Ark itself together with its staves. These cherubim, with outspread wings overshadowing the mercy seat, signify the angels who continually adore the presence of God and guard the mysteries of divine grace. # GEM

The staves by which the Ark was carried, being somewhat longer than usual, their ends were visible before the sanctuary; but if one stood slightly outside, he could not see them. The Ark remained there to the present day — that is, to the time when this book was written, before the Babylonian captivity.

There was nothing else in the Ark except the two tablets which Moses had placed there at Horeb, when the Lord gave the Law to the children of Israel as they came out of Egypt. The golden pot of manna and Aaron's rod, mentioned in Hebrews 9:4 as being in or near the Ark, were apparently placed before it rather than within it, or were removed at some earlier time.

When the priests came out of the sanctuary — for all the priests who could be found there had sanctified themselves, and the rotation of courses and order of ministries had not yet been divided among them at that time — all served together on this extraordinary occasion of dedication, without waiting for their assigned weekly course.

Both the Levites and the cantors — those under Asaph, those under Heman, and those under Idithun, together with their sons and brethren — stood at the east side of the altar, clothed in fine linen, with cymbals and psalteries and harps, and with them a hundred and twenty priests sounding trumpets. The three great choir-masters Asaph, Heman, and Idithun represent the full complement of sacred music ordered by David for the perpetual praise of God in His house.

Solomon says: "The Lord promised that He would dwell in a cloud" — referring to the dark cloud in which God appeared in the Tabernacle and Temple, signifying His hidden and incomprehensible majesty. As à Lapide expounds in parallel at 3 Reg. 8, this darkness signifies that God surpasses all human understanding.

I have built a house for His name, that He might dwell there forever. Solomon here distinguishes between the invisible divine presence and its visible, symbolic dwelling. The Temple is the house not of God's essence but of His name — i.e., of His manifestation and honor among men.

Blessed be the Lord God of Israel, who with His hands has fulfilled what He spoke with His mouth to my father David. Solomon publicly acknowledges that the promise made to David has now been completed, giving all glory to God rather than to himself as builder.

God says He chose no city from all the tribes of Israel in which to build a house from the day He brought Israel out of Egypt — meaning Jerusalem was not chosen until David's time. This shows the divine freedom in election, not bound by prior human expectation.

But I chose Jerusalem, that My name might be there, and I chose David to be over My people Israel. These two elections — of Jerusalem and of David — are paired as the foundation of the Messianic economy: the holy city and the royal line from which Christ would come.

It was in David's heart to build a house for the Lord God of Israel. À Lapide notes that the good intention of David was accepted by God as meritorious even though the act itself was reserved for Solomon. The will to do good is itself precious before God.

God tells David: "Because it was in your heart to build a house for My name, you did well to have this intention." À Lapide emphasizes that God here explicitly praises the devout will, even when the execution of the work is denied — a principle of importance for moral theology. # GEM

Nevertheless, you shall not build the house, but your son who shall come forth from your loins, he shall build the house for My name. The reason David was excluded was not sin alone but divine ordinance — the Temple of peace was to be built by the man of peace (Solomon), not the man of war.

The Lord has fulfilled His word which He spoke, and I have risen in place of David my father, and sit on the throne of Israel, as the Lord promised, and have built a house for the name of the Lord God of Israel. Solomon sees his own reign and the Temple's completion as the fulfillment of prophetic promise, not human achievement.

And I have placed in it the ark, in which is the covenant of the Lord which He made with the children of Israel. The ark containing the tablets of the covenant is the heart of the Temple — the sign of God's bond with Israel. À Lapide notes this was the only content of the ark at this time, as he explained at Heb. 9:4.

Solomon stood before the altar of the Lord in the sight of all the assembly of Israel and spread out his hands — a posture of prayer universally recognized in antiquity, the priest-king interceding before God on behalf of his people, anticipating Christ the eternal High Priest.

Solomon had made a bronze platform (basis aenea) five cubits long, five cubits wide, and three cubits high, placed it in the middle of the court, stood upon it, then knelt down before all the assembly of Israel and raised his palms to heaven. À Lapide notes this elevated platform allowed all the people to see the king praying, making his prayer a public and exemplary act of worship. Kneeling signifies humility and penitence before God.

O Lord God of Israel, there is no God like You in heaven or on earth. Solomon begins his great dedicatory prayer by proclaiming the incomparable uniqueness of God — an affirmation of pure monotheism and divine transcendence that surpasses all pagan conceptions of deity.

Blight and mildew (erugo et aurugo) are diseases of crops, when they contract, as it were, the rust of copper and gold, and take on a jaundiced, yellowed color, so that they wither and are emptied out.

Arise, O Lord, into thy rest — that is, into this temple which thou hast commanded me to build, that thou mayest rest and dwell among us continually. The rest of God, therefore, is the temple; for there he is worshipped with prayers, vows, sacrifices, and the like.
Thou and the ark of thy strength — for in the ark God was accustomed to display his power and might.

When Solomon had completed his prayers, fire descended from heaven and devoured the burnt offerings and sacrifices, and the majesty of the Lord filled the house. Lapide notes this divine fire as a sign of God's acceptance, paralleling the fire that consumed Elijah's sacrifice on Carmel, and the fire that fell on the tabernacle of Moses (Lev. 9:24), signifying that God approves and ratifies what has been consecrated to Him.

The priests could not enter the temple of the Lord because the majesty of the Lord had filled it. Lapide observes that this overwhelming divine presence rendered even the sacred ministers unable to perform their functions — so full was the temple of God's glory that human ministry was, for a time, suspended before it.

All the children of Israel saw the fire descending and the glory of the Lord upon the house, and falling prostrate on the stone pavement, they adored and praised the Lord, saying: "For He is good, for His mercy endures forever." Lapide notes the spontaneous and unanimous act of adoration as a model of true liturgical worship — the sight of divine glory draws the whole people to prostration, praise, and confession of God's eternal mercy.

The king and all the people offered sacrifices before the Lord. Lapide notes the union of king and people in sacrifice as emblematic of the whole community's participation in the dedication of the temple and in the worship of God.

Solomon slaughtered twenty-two thousand oxen and one hundred and twenty thousand rams, and thus the king and all the people dedicated the house of God. Lapide acknowledges the enormous scale of the sacrifices, interpreting them as expressing the magnitude of Solomon's gratitude and the solemnity of the occasion, though he notes that such lavish external worship must be accompanied by interior devotion to be truly pleasing to God.

The priests stood in their offices and the Levites with the musical instruments of the Lord which King David had made to praise the Lord — "for His mercy endures forever" — singing the hymns of David by their own hand; and the priests sounded trumpets before them, and all Israel stood. Lapide comments on the ordered liturgical arrangement, with priests and Levites each fulfilling their proper roles, singing and playing instruments according to the ordinances of David, as a model of hierarchical, ordered worship. # GEM

Solomon also sanctified the middle of the court before the temple of the Lord, for he had offered burnt offerings and the fat of the peace offerings there, because the bronze altar which he had made was not able to sustain the burnt offerings, the sacrifices, and the fat. Lapide explains that the sheer volume of sacrifices exceeded even the great bronze altar's capacity, requiring the entire court to be consecrated for sacred use, illustrating the extraordinary scale of the dedication.

Solomon observed the feast at that time for seven days, and all Israel with him — a very great assembly, from the entrance of Hamath to the torrent of Egypt. Lapide notes that the extent of the gathering, from Hamath in the north to the brook of Egypt in the south, represents the full breadth of Israel's territory, signifying the unity of the entire nation in this act of solemn dedication and celebration.

On the eighth day they held a solemn assembly, because they had kept the dedication of the altar for seven days and the feast for seven days. Lapide observes the significance of the eighth day assembly, the collecta, as a conclusion and seal of the entire celebration, noting that the number eight traditionally signifies a new beginning or the age to come, as Augustine and others have remarked.

On the twenty-third day of the seventh month he dismissed the people to their tents, rejoicing and glad of heart over the good that the Lord had done for David, for Solomon, and for His people Israel. Lapide emphasizes the joy of the people as a fitting response to divine beneficence, noting that true religion is not merely external observance but produces interior gladness rooted in gratitude for God's goodness toward His people.

Solomon completed the house of the Lord and the king's house, and all that he had purposed in his heart to do in the house of the Lord and in his own house; and he prospered. Lapide notes that the completion and prosperity of Solomon's works is attributed entirely to divine blessing, since God crowns with success those undertakings which are ordered to His glory.

The Lord appeared to him by night and said: "I have heard your prayer and have chosen this place for Myself as a house of sacrifice." Lapide observes that God appears to Solomon at night, as He had done before at Gibeon (1 Kings 3), suggesting that divine revelations often come in the stillness of night when the soul is recollected and withdrawn from external distractions, following the Augustinian principle that God speaks to the interior man. # GEM

If I shut up heaven so that there be no rain, or if I command the locust to devour the land, or if I send pestilence among My people. Lapide notes that God here enumerates three classic biblical punishments — drought, locusts, and pestilence — which were the standard divine chastisements for sin in Israel, citing Jerome and others who see these as representing the full range of temporal afflictions by which God corrects His wayward people.

And My people, upon whom My name is called, being converted, shall make supplication to Me, and seek My face, and do penance for their most wicked ways: then I will hear from heaven, and will forgive their sins and will heal their land. Lapide regards this verse as among the most consoling promises in all of Scripture, laying out the conditions of divine forgiveness with great clarity: conversion, prayer, seeking God's face, and penance. He notes that Augustine and Chrysostom both see here a universal pattern of divine mercy — that no matter how grave the sin or how severe the punishment, sincere repentance always opens the way to pardon and restoration. # GEM

My eyes also shall be open and My ears attentive to the prayer that is made in this place. Lapide explains that God's open eyes and attentive ears are anthropomorphic expressions signifying His most perfect and vigilant care for those who pray in the temple, noting that what was said of the Jerusalem temple is fulfilled most perfectly in the Church, which is the true house of prayer, as the Fathers universally teach.

After twenty years of building the Temple and his own palace, Solomon turned to further works of construction and administration. Lapide refers readers to his fuller treatment of these matters in his commentary on 3 Kings (1 Kings) chapters 8 and 9.
The cities which Hiram had given to Solomon, Solomon rebuilt and settled the children of Israel in them. Lapide notes the exchange between Solomon and Hiram as background to this repopulation.
Solomon went to Hamath-zobah and took possession of it, extending his dominion northward.
He built Tadmor (Palmyra) in the desert, and built other strongly fortified cities in Hamath, demonstrating the breadth of his building program across the wilderness regions.
He built Upper Beth-horon and Lower Beth-horon as walled cities with gates, bars, and bolts — key strategic fortifications guarding the approaches to Jerusalem.

He built Baalath and all the fortified cities that belonged to Solomon — cities for chariots, cities for horsemen — whatever Solomon desired to build in Jerusalem, Lebanon, and throughout all the land of his dominion.
All the remnants of the Hittites, Amorites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites who were not of the stock of Israel —

their descendants, whom the children of Israel had not destroyed, Solomon subjected as tribute-bearers unto that day. Lapide observes that Solomon reduced these Canaanite remnants to servitude rather than exterminating them, fulfilling the spirit of the earlier conquest.

But of the children of Israel, Solomon made none servants for the king's work, for they were men of war, chief captains, and commanders of his chariots and horsemen. Lapide emphasizes the distinction between the free Israelites serving as soldiers and nobles, and the subjected Canaanites serving as laborers.
All the chief officers of King Solomon's army numbered two hundred and fifty, who had authority over the people. These were the administrators and disciplinarians of the workforce and military.

Solomon brought Pharaoh's daughter up from the City of David to the house he had built for her. The king said: My wife shall not dwell in the house of David king of Israel, for it is holy, because the ark of the Lord has entered it. Lapide notes Solomon's religious scruple — a pagan woman was deemed unworthy to inhabit the sanctified space where the Ark had dwelt — showing Solomon's reverence for the holiness associated with David's city.
Then Solomon offered burnt offerings to the Lord upon the altar of the Lord which he had built before the porch.

The altar was appointed for daily offerings according to the commandment of Moses, on sabbaths, new moons, and the three annual feasts: the feast of Unleavened Bread, the feast of Weeks, and the feast of Tabernacles. Lapide notes this faithful observance of the Mosaic liturgical calendar as evidence of Solomon's piety in ordering Temple worship.

Solomon appointed, according to the order of David his father, the offices of the priests in their service, and the Levites in their watches — to praise and minister before the priests day by day — and the porters in their divisions at every gate. Lapide notes that the Hebrew reads "by their guards" (per custodias suas), meaning the priests were divided into their courses according to the offices individually assigned them by David. The Levites stood at their musical instruments (organis carminum), singing hymns with their hands — that is, playing the musical instruments with their hands, chanting psalms composed by David for the praise of God.

[Synopsis] Lapide provides a chapter synopsis: Solomon builds his palace and many cities; subjugates the Canaanite remnants; ordains fixed sacrifices to be offered at royal expense; distributes the offices of priests and Levites according to David's arrangement; and sends a fleet to Ophir, from which he receives 450 talents of gold.

God declares: I have chosen and sanctified this place that my name may be there forever, and my eyes and my heart shall be there perpetually. Lapide treats this divine promise as the foundation of the Temple's perpetual sacred character.
God addresses Solomon conditionally: If you walk before me as David your father walked, and do according to all I have commanded you, keeping my statutes and judgments —

then I will establish the throne of your kingdom as I promised David your father, saying: There shall not fail you a man to be ruler in Israel. Lapide sees this as the conditional Davidic covenant, linking Solomon's obedience to the perpetuity of the dynasty.
But if you turn away and forsake my statutes and commandments which I have set before you, and go and serve other gods and worship them —
then I will uproot you from my land which I have given you, and this house which I have sanctified for my name I will cast out of my sight, and will make it a proverb and a byword among all nations.

And this house, which is so high, shall be an astonishment to everyone who passes by; they shall say: Why has the Lord done thus to this land and to this house? And it shall be answered: Because they forsook the Lord God of their fathers who brought them out of Egypt, and embraced other gods and worshipped and served them — therefore he has brought all this evil upon them. Lapide notes the pedagogical function of the Temple's desolation as a public testimony to divine justice among all peoples.
The Hebrew word for "majesty" (כָּבוֹד, cabod) means glory — specifically a luminous, glorious cloud representing the invisible majesty and glory of God. # GEM
The Queen of Sheba came to Solomon with great wealth, camels bearing spices, much gold, and precious gems, to test him with enigmas (riddles) in Jerusalem. She spoke to him all that was in her heart.
Solomon explained to her everything she had proposed; there was nothing he did not make clear to her.
When she had seen Solomon's wisdom and the house he had built,

as well as the food of his table, the dwellings of his servants, the offices of his ministers, their garments, the cupbearers and their vestments, and the sacrifices he offered in the house of the Lord — she was left without spirit for amazement (stupore).

She said to the king: "The report I heard in my own land about your deeds and your wisdom is true." The Hebrew, Septuagint, and Chaldean read "your words" (de verbis tuis), meaning things accomplished by you or pertaining to you.
I did not believe those who told me until I came and my own eyes saw it; and scarcely half of your wisdom was told to me — you have surpassed the fame by your deeds.
Blessed are your men and blessed are your servants who stand before you continually and hear your wisdom.

Blessed be the Lord your God, who willed to set you upon His throne as king of the Lord your God. Because God loves Israel and wishes to preserve it forever, He placed you as king over them to execute judgment and justice.
She gave the king one hundred twenty talents of gold, and very many spices, and most precious gems; never had there been spices such as those which the Queen of Sheba gave to King Solomon.
The servants of Hiram together with the servants of Solomon also brought gold from Ophir, and thyine wood and most precious gems.
From the thyine wood the king made steps for the house of the Lord and for the royal palace, and also harps and psalteries for the singers; such wood had never been seen in the land of Judah.
King Solomon gave to the Queen of Sheba all she desired and asked for, and much more than she had brought to him; and she returned to her own land with her servants.
The weight of gold that was brought to Solomon each year was six hundred sixty-six talents of gold,
besides what ambassadors of various nations and merchants customarily brought, as well as all the kings of Arabia and the satraps of the lands, who conveyed gold and silver to Solomon.
King Solomon made two hundred golden spears, six hundred pieces of gold being spent on each spear.

He also made three hundred golden shields, three hundred pieces of gold covering each shield; and the king placed them in the armory that was surrounded by a grove — that is, in the house of the Forest of Lebanon, as the Septuagint renders it. The three hundred and six hundred pieces of gold for the shields and spears were shekels, each worth four drachmas, i.e., four crowns, as shown in 3 Kings 10:16.
The king also made a great throne of ivory and overlaid it with the purest gold.
There were also six steps by which one ascended to the throne, a golden footstool, and two armrests on either side, and two lions standing beside the armrests,
and twelve other lions standing on the six steps on either side; no such throne existed in any kingdom.
All the vessels of the king's banquet were of gold, and the vessels of the house of the Forest of Lebanon were of the purest gold. Silver in those days was reckoned as nothing.

For the king's ships went to Tarshish with the servants of Hiram once every three years, and brought back gold and silver, ivory, apes, and peacocks. The Hebrews called the sea and overseas regions — especially distant and unknown ones such as Ophir and India — "Tarshish." See what was said at 3 Kings 9:28 and Jonah 1:3.
Solomon was thus magnified above all the kings of the earth in riches and glory.
All the kings of the earth desired to see Solomon's face, to hear the wisdom which God had placed in his heart,
and they brought him gifts: silver and golden vessels, garments, weapons, spices, horses and mules, year by year.
Solomon had forty thousand horses in his stables, and twelve thousand horsemen and charioteers, and he stationed them in the chariot cities and with the king in Jerusalem.
He also exercised power over all kings from the river Euphrates to the land of the Philistines and to the borders of Egypt.
He made silver as plentiful in Jerusalem as stones, and cedars as numerous as the sycamores that grow in the plains.
Horses were brought to him from Egypt and from all regions.
The rest of Solomon's acts, first and last, are written in the words of Nathan the Prophet, in the books of Ahijah the Shilonite, and in the vision of Iddo the Seer against Jeroboam son of Nebat.
Solomon reigned in Jerusalem over all Israel for forty years.
He slept with his fathers and was buried in the City of David; and Rehoboam his son reigned in his place. Lapide notes that this entire chapter was explained in his commentary on 3 Kings 10.
Chosen men and warriors (electorum atque bellantium) is a hendiadys. The Hebrew bachurim means both "chosen" and "young men." As the saying goes: wars belong to the young, counsel to the old.

He placed commanders in them — that is, military leaders with sufficient provisions, so that the cities on the border between Judah and Israel could withstand the attacks of the nearby enemy, namely Jeroboam his adversary.
Because Jeroboam had cast them out — that is, he deprived them of the priestly and Levitical office, because they refused to serve his calves as idols, but wished to serve the true God.
They walked in the ways of David and Solomon — understand: before Solomon in his old age apostatized from God to idols.
He appointed Abijah as chief — that is, head and prince over the other brothers, who numbered 28, because although he was younger in age, he surpassed all in prudence, vigor, and industry.

And mightier above all his sons, and in all the borders of Judah and Benjamin, and in all the fortified cities — supply: Abijah was mightier. The meaning is that Abijah occupied all the borders of the kingdom and all the fortified and strengthened cities, making himself lord and powerful over all, since all the borders and fortified cities were obedient to him.

And he provided them [the fortified cities and borders] with abundant food (lest enemies besiege them and force surrender by famine), "and sought many wives" — namely Abijah for himself, to beget many sons by them, by whom he might establish and strengthen his kingdom against invasion by his brothers. This sense is required by our version as well as the Septuagint. Alternatively, from the Hebrew with Pagninus, Vatablus, Marinus and others: Rehoboam dispersed all his sons over all the borders of Judah and Benjamin and through all the fortified cities — so that they would be separated from one another, unable to put their heads together and conspire against Abijah the younger whom he had set over them all — following that political and tyrannical maxim of Machiavelli: "If you wish to reign, divide." And that they might live content with their lot and not think of the throne, he gave them abundant food and many wives, so that, devoted to belly and pleasure, they might grow torpid and grow old in delightful idleness. The Turkish Emperor does the same with his brothers until he has offspring; once he has them, he kills his brothers lest they seize the empire.

My fury shall not be poured out upon Jerusalem — that is, any further through the continuous slaughters, plunderings, and devastations that Shishak was preparing. Note here how quickly God is appeased by even a partial repentance such as that of Rehoboam and the Jews. Yet it is clear that their repentance was neither truly sincere and earnest, nor firm and stable, as is evident from the fact that Rehoboam, under the instigation of his mother Naamah the Ammonitess — and therefore an idolatress — turned back to idols, as is stated in verses 13 and 14.

In the eighteenth year of King Jeroboam, Abijah reigned over Judah for three years in Jerusalem. His mother's name was Michaiah, daughter of Uriel of Gibeah. There was war between Abijah and Jeroboam.
Abijah entered into battle with four hundred thousand chosen warriors; Jeroboam arrayed against him eight hundred thousand men, themselves also chosen and mighty in war.
Abijah stood upon Mount Zemaraim, which is in Ephraim, and delivered his speech to Jeroboam and all Israel.

Do you not know that the LORD God of Israel gave the kingdom over Israel to David forever, to him and to his sons by a covenant of salt? — that is, a perpetual and inviolable covenant, for salt preserves from corruption and signifies everlasting firmness.
Jeroboam the son of Nebat, a servant of Solomon the son of David, rose up and rebelled against his lord — note that rebellion against a lawful sovereign is here condemned as a grave sin.

Worthless men and sons of Belial gathered to Jeroboam and prevailed against Rehoboam the son of Solomon, for Rehoboam was young and faint-hearted and could not resist them — showing that weakness of character and want of courage in a ruler opens the door to sedition.

You say that you can resist the kingdom of the LORD, which he possesses through the sons of David, and you have the golden calves which Jeroboam made for you as gods — Abijah reproaches Israel for both political rebellion and idolatry combined.

Israel had cast out the priests of the LORD, the sons of Aaron, and the Levites, and made priests after the manner of the nations: whoever came and consecrated his hand with a young bull and seven rams became a priest of those who are no gods — a sharp condemnation of illicit and self-appointed priesthood.

But as for us, the LORD is our God, whom we have not forsaken, and priests of the sons of Aaron minister to the LORD, and the Levites are in their order — Abijah contrasts the legitimate worship of Judah with the schismatic worship of Israel.

They offer to the LORD burnt offerings every morning and evening, incense compounded according to the prescription of the law, the showbread is set out on the pure table, the golden lampstand with its lamps burns every evening — all the rites of the law are punctually observed in Judah, which is proof of God's favor.

God himself is our leader in battle, and his priests with their trumpets sound the alarm against you: O children of Israel, do not fight against the LORD God of your fathers, for it will not prosper you — a solemn warning that to fight against those who have God on their side is to fight against God himself.

While Abijah was still speaking, Jeroboam was arranging an ambush behind him; while he stood facing the enemy, he surrounded Judah with his army without their knowledge — showing the treachery of the schismatic as contrasted with the frank speech of Abijah.

Judah looked and saw the battle was both before and behind them; they cried to the LORD and the priests blew the trumpets — prayer and sacred rites are the proper recourse in desperate military situations.

All the men of Judah shouted, and God struck Jeroboam and all Israel with terror before Abijah and Judah — the victory was entirely God's work, triggered by the cry of prayer and the sound of the sacred trumpets.
The children of Israel fled before Judah, and God delivered them into their hand — divine intervention, not human strength, decided the battle.

Abijah and his people struck them with a great slaughter; five hundred thousand chosen men of Israel fell slain — an enormous number, signifying the magnitude of God's judgment upon the schismatic kingdom that had abandoned true worship.

The children of Israel were humbled at that time, and the children of Judah were greatly strengthened because they had trusted in the LORD God of their fathers — trust in God is the true source of military and political strength.

Abijah pursued Jeroboam and took from him Bethel and its villages, Jeshanah and its villages, and Ephron and its villages — the victory was followed by territorial gains, a temporal reward for fidelity to God.

Jeroboam was not able to prevail again in the days of Abijah; the LORD struck him and he died — divine punishment overtook Jeroboam, the author of the schism and the golden calves, as a fitting end to his rebellion.
Abia, having consolidated his reign, took fourteen wives and fathered twenty-two sons and sixteen daughters.
The remaining acts of Abia, his ways and works, are recorded most carefully in the Book of the Prophet Iddo.
Michah daughter of Uriel: see what was said at 3 Kings (1 Kings) 15.

Abia stood upon the mountain so that his oration could be heard by all the Israelites encamped round about. This speech is pious and effective, by which he attempts by many arguments to persuade them to abandon their novel religion and king, and return to the ancestral God and the kingdom of David in Jerusalem — and above all because he has God fighting for him, while they, as idolaters, have God against them. It is as if to say: You act madly if you wish to fight against God. Read and weigh it carefully.

In a covenant of salt means a firm, stable, and eternal covenant. For salt preserves things intact and safe from corruption; hence salt is a symbol of eternity, as noted at Leviticus 2:12. Our author Sanchez adds: It is clear that this covenant concerning the kingdom in David's family was perpetually conditional — namely, if David's posterity keeps the covenant (Psalm 88:30). God therefore gave the kingdom to David's house with a grain of salt, that is, conditionally — if they do not depart from the faith they were obliged to keep whole, or if David's posterity applies the salt and does not allow the struck covenant to be corrupted and dissolved.

Whoever fills his hand (Hebrew and Septuagint: "filled his hand") with a young bull (Hebrew: a calf, son of a cow) and seven rams "becomes a priest of those who are not gods." That is: as you have false gods, so also you have false priests; for God did not choose them as He chose Aaron and his descendants, but whoever — however base and wicked — consecrated his own hands by offering a bull and seven rams, by that very act became a priest of the idols, namely the golden calves. Note: "to fill the hand" in Hebrew is equivalent to "to consecrate a priest," for by filling the hands with sacrificial victims and offering them, one consecrated oneself as priest. This was the rite of priestly consecration, as is clear from Leviticus 8.

From this passage and the following, where mention is made of only one table and one lampstand, Torniellus concludes that Shishak king of Egypt, when he plundered the treasures of the Temple, left of the ten tables and ten lampstands which King Solomon had placed in the Temple nothing but one table and one lampstand, and that the Jews were content with these thereafter, just as they had been before the Temple was built by Solomon.

Whom the Lord struck, and he died — namely Jeroboam, for the preceding discourse concerned him; Jeroboam perished miserably after two or three years. The Hebrews however in the Seder Olam understand not Jeroboam but Abijah himself, saying he was ungrateful to God and did not give worthy thanks for so great a victory, and therefore died shortly afterward, having reigned only three years and having died two years before Jeroboam. But this does not accord well with what immediately follows: "Then Abijah, having consolidated his reign, took fourteen wives," etc.
Abia slept with his fathers and was buried in the City of David; and his son Asa reigned in his place, in whose days the land was at peace for ten years.
Asa did what was good and pleasing in the sight of his God, and overthrew the altars of foreign worship and the high places.
He broke down the standing pillars and cut down the sacred groves.
He commanded Judah to seek the Lord God of their fathers and to observe the law and all the commandments.
He removed from all the cities of Judah the altars and shrines, and he reigned in peace.
He also built fortified cities in Judah, for the land was at rest and no wars arose in his time, the Lord granting peace.

He said to Judah: Let us build these cities and surround them with walls and strengthen them with towers, gates, and bars, while all things are quiet from wars, because we have sought the Lord our God and He has given us peace on every side. They built therefore, and there was no obstacle in the building.
Asa had in his army from Judah three hundred thousand bearing shields and spears, and from Benjamin two hundred and eighty thousand shield-bearers and archers — all these were very valiant men.
Zerah the Ethiopian came out against them with a million soldiers.

King Asa destroyed the altars of foreign worship (by which the gentiles venerated their gods and idols) and the HIGH PLACES. One may object that 1 Kings 15:14 says "he did not remove the high places." The answer is that some high places were dedicated to idols, others to the true God: Asa removed the former but not the latter. Again, Asa removed the altars, images, and groves of idols, but not all the temples dedicated to them by Solomon; these were later removed by Josiah (2 Kings 23:13).

And he took away the shrines — namely of Baal, Astaroth, and the other gods. The Hebrew is Chammonim or hammonim, which properly signifies "sun-temples," that is, shrines or altars of the Sun or Ammon. For in Punic, Ammon or Jupiter Ammonius means the Sun, derived from the Hebrew chamah or hammah, meaning the Sun, so called from heat, since chamam means "to make warm." See what was said on Ezekiel 6:6.

Now there went out against them Zara the Ethiopian with his army (in approximately the fifteenth year of Asa's reign, as gathered from the following chapter v.10; so Salianus) "of a hundred thousand men." Regarding "Ethiopian": Torniellus and others cited by Genebrard take Ethiopia here to mean Midian, from which region Moses' wife is called an Ethiopian (Num. 12). Their reasoning is that the Ethiopians properly so called are far distant from Judea. But Serarius, Sanchez, Salianus, and others more correctly take this to mean true Ethiopia, which lies beneath Egypt. For this Ethiopia is adjacent to Egypt and thus near to Judea; and perhaps Zara was king of both Egypt and Ethiopia.

Azariah son of Oded, upon whom the Spirit of God came, went out to meet Asa and declared God's promise of presence conditional upon faithfulness: "The Lord is with you, because you were with Him." Lapide notes the prophetic pattern here — divine accompaniment follows human fidelity.

The prophet declares the fundamental principle of covenant relationship: seeking God guarantees finding Him, but abandoning God results in being abandoned by Him. Lapide sees this as a universal axiom of divine justice applicable to all ages.

The prophecy foretells that Israel will for a long time be deprived of the true God, of priest, teacher, and law. Lapide interprets this as fulfilled during the period of the divided kingdom and apostasy, when the northern tribes were cut off from legitimate worship, priesthood, and Torah observance.

Yet when in their tribulation they return to the Lord God of Israel and seek Him, they will find Him. Lapide emphasizes God's perpetual readiness to receive the penitent — tribulation serves as the instrument of conversion. # GEM

In that time there will be no peace for those going out or coming in, but terrors on every side among all the inhabitants of the lands. Lapide connects this to the civil strife and constant warfare that plagued the divided kingdoms as a consequence of apostasy.

Nation will fight against nation and city against city, for God will trouble them with every kind of distress. Lapide notes that God here uses political and military chaos as instruments of chastisement, stirring up conflict among peoples as a direct result of their abandonment of true worship.

But as for you, be strong and do not let your hands be weak, for your work shall be rewarded. Lapide interprets this as the prophet's exhortation to Asa to persevere in reform, promising that no act of piety toward God goes unrewarded — divine justice requites faithful labor.

The Ethiopians fell to the point of annihilation because the Lord struck them down and fought on behalf of Asa, with either the king's army or, as Lapide suggests, the army of God's holy angels engaged in battle for Judah. Lapide cites Theodore (lib. 3) who identifies these enemies as Libyans, whom the Septuagint calls Amazons, confirming they were African Ethiopians and Libyans, not the Midianite peoples of Asia — further confirmed by the fact that Asa recovered the spoils that Shishak king of Egypt had taken from Judah 26 years earlier under Rehoboam.

Many days without the true God, without a teacher, and without the law — this refers to the present time when Jeroboam introduced the worship of the golden calves into Israel; for all his successors and every king of Israel followed him in this calf-worship, and from the kings of Judah, Joram, Ochozias, Athalia, Achaz, Manasse, Joakim, and Sedecia likewise imitated them, worshipping idols in place of the ancestral and true God.

When they returned in their distress to the Lord God of Israel, they found Him. Thus the penitent Achab found God, and Joas to whom Eliseus predicted a triple victory over the Syrians, and the penitent Manasse, and likewise the pious Ezechias, Josias, and others like them.

In the third month — that is, Sivan, in which they celebrated Pentecost — indicating that by invoking God at this feast they obtained the victory through His aid, and therefore offered the spoils of their enemies to Him in thanksgiving. Conversely, Constantinople was taken by the Turk at Pentecost, because the Greeks held impious views concerning the Holy Spirit, whose feast this is, teaching that He does not proceed from the Father and the Son.

Whoever does not seek the Lord God of Israel, let him die, from the least to the greatest, from man to woman. This decree established death as the penalty for apostasy from the covenant being renewed.

But the heart of Asa was perfect with the Lord all his days — this refers to the true worship of God; that is, Asa worshipped the true God throughout his entire life and never turned aside to idols as others did. For in other respects Asa sinned and gravely offended God, as will be evident in the following chapter at verses 7, 10, and 12.

Until the thirty-fifth year — this is the correct reading with the Hebrew, Septuagint, and Roman editions, not "the thirtieth" as some read — "of the reign of Asa," meaning the reign of Judah, which began properly at the death of Solomon when it was separated from the kingdom of Israel by the schism of Jeroboam; so that the first year of Roboam was the first year of the kingdom of Judah as distinct from the kingdom of Israel, which also began at the same time under its first king Jeroboam. So the Hebrew, Sanchez, Salian, Tornielus, and others; this will be more evident at the next chapter, verse 1.

On "In the thirty-sixth year of his (Asa's) reign, Baasha went up": An objector notes that Baasha had already been dead for a decade, namely since the 25th year of Asa's reign, as is clear from 3 Kings 16:8; for in the 26th year of Asa, Omri was reigning in Israel (3 Kings 16:22). Cajetan, Johannes Lucidus, Adrichomius in his Chronology, Johannes Annius with his pseudo-Philo, and Melchior Canus (lib. 11, de locis, c. 5, ad 12 arg.) respond first that a scribal error crept in here, and that instead of 36 one should read 26, or rather 16. This would not be surprising, since the matter does not pertain to faith or morals. However, all Hebrew, Chaldean, Greek, and Latin codices consistently read 36, not 26; to say that all codices contain an error is to say that Scripture contains an error, which is far from acceptable.

Second, Lyra, Vatablus, and Genebrard respond that the years of Asa are counted in two ways: first, from the beginning of his reign; second, from the memorable Ethiopian war and victory. Hence the 36th year of Asa's reign would be the 26th year from the Ethiopian war, at which time Ela is said to have succeeded the dying Baasha (3 Kings 16:8). But this solution disturbs the entire chronology of Sacred Scripture, for by the same logic the years of the subsequent kings — Zimri, Omri, Ahab — who reigned alongside Asa would have to be numbered from the Ethiopian war, which no one would assert.

Third, others say these 36 years are years of Asa's life, not of his reign. But this is contradicted by the text, which expressly says "in the 36th year of the reign," not of his life. Hence Abulensis responds that another solution must be sought here, one which he himself confesses he does not know.

Fourth, and most correctly, one may say that these 36 years are years of the kingdom of Judah, and therefore are to be reckoned from the first year of Rehoboam, who was the first king of Judah when Israel — that is, the ten tribes — made Jeroboam their king. At that point a new era was established, and many began to count years from this notable schism, since the kingdom of Judah was then instituted, just as the kingdom of Israel was. Hence throughout Scripture the years of the kings of Israel are numbered according to the years of the kings of Judah who reigned alongside them. Therefore: Rehoboam, first king of Judah, reigned 17 years; then Abijah, 3 years; then Asa, 16 years — add all of these together and you have the 36 years in question. Thus this 36th year of the kingdom of Judah was precisely the 16th year of Asa's reign. If one objects that the 36 years here are called years of Asa's reign, not of Judah's, the answer is that Asa's kingdom and Judah's kingdom are the same thing, for Asa had no kingdom other than Judah. Therefore when you reckon the years of the kingdom of Judah, you are reckoning the years of Asa, who presided over it and whose kingdom it was. So hold the Seder Olam (i.e., the Hebrew Chronicle), Torniellus, Salianus, and Azorius (tom. 1).

Asa brought out silver and gold from the treasuries of the house of the Lord and from the royal treasuries, and sent them to Benhadad king of Syria who dwelt in Damascus. This narrative corresponds to 3 Kings 15:18.

At that time Hanani the prophet came to Asa king of Judah and said to him: "Because you put your trust in the king of Syria and not in the Lord your God, therefore the army of the king of Syria has escaped from your hand." The prophet rebukes Asa for having sought help from a foreign king rather than from God, thereby losing the opportunity to defeat Syria as well.

Were not the Ethiopians and Libyans far more numerous, with chariots and horsemen and a very great multitude — yet because you trusted in the Lord, He delivered them into your hand? The prophet recalls the victory over the Ethiopians (described in ch. 14) to show that God's power is sufficient and that trust in Him yields victory even against overwhelming odds. # GEM

For the eyes of the Lord run to and fro throughout the whole earth, to give strength to those whose heart is wholly devoted to Him. You have acted foolishly in this, and from now on you shall have wars. This verse is a celebrated declaration of divine providence: God actively surveys all creation to strengthen those of perfect heart, and Asa's failure of faith will bring perpetual warfare upon him. # GEM

Asa was enraged at the seer and had him put in prison, for he was greatly angered at him for this. And Asa oppressed some of the people at that time. Thus the king, stung by prophetic rebuke, turned to tyranny — imprisoning God's messenger and persecuting the people — compounding his sin of distrust with sins of cruelty.

In the thirty-ninth year of his reign Asa was diseased in his feet — a very severe illness — yet even in his disease he did not seek the Lord, but relied on physicians. Lapide notes this as a further mark of Asa's decline: the text does not condemn the use of physicians as such, but rather his exclusive reliance on them to the complete neglect of God.
And Asa slept with his fathers and died in the forty-first year of his reign.

They buried him in the tomb which he had hewn for himself in the city of David. They laid him on a bier filled with spices and various kinds of blended ointments prepared by the perfumers' art, and they made a very great fire in his honor. Lapide notes that this honorable burial with aromatic spices and a great fire lit in his honor reflects the ancient royal funeral rites of Judah.

Cedrenus and Suidas report that this book was removed/suppressed by Hezekiah, on two grounds: first, because the people were seeking healing from physicians while neglecting and not invoking God; second, because they were perversely seeking the physicians' help before seeking God's appeasement and assistance.

And his heart was lifted up in the ways of the Lord — that is, Jehoshaphat was made bold by piety, religion, and keeping of the divine law, so that he feared neither subjects nor enemies, but trusting in God he boldly cut down the high places and groves which other pious kings, fearing the offense or rebellion of the people, had tolerated and permitted. The Hebrew and Septuagint read: "And his heart was exalted in the ways of the Lord," meaning, as Vatablus says, that nothing was more important to him than observing the commandments of the Lord; or rather, that Jehoshaphat conceived great and lofty courage from God and the worship of God, so that, fearing no one and undaunted, he abolished the high places and resolved in his heart to do other heroic works for promoting the honor and glory of God. See here what piety and pious intention accomplish — they fear nothing and dare all things, with God's favor. Such in this age was Pope Pius V, who relying on God feared not kings, but was himself the terror and scourge of kings and even of the Turks. For holiness is a terror to all, as much as it is an object of reverence. # GEM

He sent from his princes — that is, the princes of his court, who by his command, having abolished idols, restored the true worship of the one God and the observance of the Mosaic law. For this reason he sent with them Levites and Priests to teach the people the laws of the Lord. This was a remarkable zeal of King Jehoshaphat, which was once imitated by St. Stephen the first king of Hungary, St. Louis king of the Franks, St. Wenceslaus duke of Bohemia, and in our own age by the kings of Spain and Portugal, who at great expense subjugated both the Indies so that through religious men they might bring their inhabitants to the knowledge and worship of the true God.

He built in Judah houses like towers — in Hebrew, palaces so lofty and fortified that they appeared as towers. He did this for both the adornment and the defense of Judah. Just as the Burgundians against the incursion of Vandals, Alans, Goths, etc., built πύργους, that is, burghs and towers; from which they were called Burgundiones.

Amasiah consecrated to the Lord — Hebrew hammithnadab, meaning, as Vatablus explains, one who offered himself voluntarily to the Lord, that is, devoted himself to holy warfare, to fight for God and the worship of God against idols and idolaters, and on behalf of the faithful and pious.

Jehoshaphat had under his command a total of 1,160,000 fighting men, God prospering him, because in addition to the powerful and numerous tribes of Judah and Benjamin subject to him, many from the ten tribes who were zealous for true religion and attracted by Jehoshaphat's prosperity came to Jerusalem.

Josaphat was rich and greatly honored, and he allied himself with Ahab by giving his son Joram Ahab's daughter Athaliah as wife, as is clear from ch. 21:6. This was the one sin of Josaphat — that he mixed society and marriage with the impious Ahab. For Athaliah, daughter of Ahab, introduced idolatry into Judah, and having killed all the king's sons, made herself queen.

There came forth a spirit — that is, the devil. The Rabbis fable that this spirit was the soul of Naboth, unjustly stoned by Jezebel, as though he here avenged himself and arranged for Ahab to be seduced by the false prophets so that he would go to the battle in which he was to be killed.

And to Joash the son of Amelech: so also Pagninus. The Hebrew has ben hammetech, which the Septuagint properly renders "son of the king." From this it appears that this Joash was a son of Ahab, to whom, as he set out to war against the Syrians, Ahab entrusted the guardianship of Samaria.

Hear, all peoples — mark well each word of my oracle, so that when shortly after you see each thing fulfilled, you may know that I was sent by God to foretell these things; and therefore that Ahab's death came not by chance but by God's vengeance; so that you may beware of his idols and crimes and return to the God of Judah.

The king of Israel stood in his chariot against the Syrians until evening — for although Ahab was wounded, he nevertheless remained in his chariot to encourage his men in the battle against the Syrians, until at evening his spirit and life failed him along with his blood.

The prophet Jehu rebukes Josaphat for giving aid to the impious Ahab and joining in friendship and alliance with those who hate the Lord — specifically through the marriage of Josaphat's son Joram to Athaliah, daughter of the wicked Ahab. Lapide observes that it is unworthy to make friends of those who are enemies of God and whose God is an enemy, unless necessity or great utility compels it; and the outcome proved this perilous, for Athaliah corrupted both Joram and the Judeans. He cites David in Psalm 138 (139):21, who professes himself an enemy of God's enemies: "Do I not hate those who hate thee, O Lord, and loathe those who rise against thee? I hate them with perfect hatred; they have become my enemies."

Though Josaphat merited the Lord's anger (chastisement and sharp punishment), yet good works were found in him — namely works of piety and zeal in overthrowing idolatry and in promoting the worship of God — on account of which God forgives him all guilt and penalty.

You exercise not the judgment of man but of God — that is, you are vicars not so much of me as of God, appointed by God through me as judges of His people to judge them justly and holily; therefore God Himself will demand a strict account of justice either rendered or neglected, and will reward or punish according to merits. Lapide exhorts princes and judges to reflect on this often: that they exercise God's judgment, and therefore must judge before God truly and sincerely, just as God Himself would judge, and as He will show them they ought to have judged on the day of judgment. Whoever keeps this in mind will not let himself be bent from the right path of justice by favor, friendship, gifts, or threats. # GEM

There is no iniquity with the Lord our God — that is, as the Septuagint renders it, no injustice; the Hebrew word ʿawlah means perversity, or the perversion and overturning of justice, whether in accepting persons or accepting bribes, as the following words make clear. So Vatablus.

The sons of Ammon, and with them some of the Ammonites — that is, of the Edomites (Idumeans). For these, lest they appear to fight against the Jews their brothers, called themselves Ammonites. So says the Author of the Traditions cited by St. Jerome here: "By Ammonites he means the Idumeans to be understood, who out of reverence for their ancestral name (Esau, who was the brother of Jacob, father of the Idumeans), were unwilling to take up arms against Israel in their usual guise; but transformed themselves into the appearance of Ammonites."

From those places which are beyond the sea — namely the Dead Sea, or the Lake Asphaltitis; and crossing it they encamped at Engedi, that is, at the borders of the kingdom of Judah. For Syria was separated from Judah by the Dead Sea, as Vatablus notes.

The new court — that is, renovated, or newly added to the temple. For when Solomon had made the temple courts exceedingly large to hold the entire people of the twelve tribes, and then the ten tribes had made their schism from Rehoboam and the temple, many parts of the court were closed off as empty or assigned to other uses. But under Jehoshaphat, as the multitude of the faithful grew — both the people of Judah and those who had defected from the ten tribes to Judah — it was necessary to reopen and renovate some of these areas to accommodate so great a people. Hence "the new court" means renovated and newly accommodated for the uses of the temple and the people.

But since we do not know what we ought to do, we have only this left: to direct our eyes to You. Note this most pious and most useful counsel of Jehoshaphat. As Philo wrote to Gaius: "When you are destitute of all human help, divine help is near, if you invoke it." For it is God's own property to come to the aid of those in straits and to restore what is despaired of; for it is precisely there that He shows His clemency and power most fully. This work therefore, as it is fitting to divinity, so also is it proper to God. # GEM

All Judah stood before the Lord with their little ones, their wives, and their children. Here is evident the antiquity of the Litany, or public and communal prayer, by which in public calamity all persons of every sex and age gather publicly, so that with one voice, and as it were with the united lamentation of all, they implore God's mercy and help, and as it were do violence to God. "For this," says Tertullian in the Apology, "is a violence pleasing to God." The Ninevites did the same (Jonah 3:5), and the Maccabees (2 Macc. 3:18ff.), and the Jews in the time of Judith (ch. 4:9). This is what Joel commands (ch. 2:16): "Gather the elders, assemble the little children and those that suck at the breasts." St. Gregory did the same during the plague that was devastating Rome and Italy: he proclaimed public Litanies of all orders, distributed them into various processions, and as all implored God's help, God stopped the plague, as his Life records.
Judah, coming to the watchtower that looks out upon the wilderness, saw the entire wide region covered with corpses, and no one surviving who had been able to escape death.

Jehoshaphat and all the people came to strip the spoils from the dead. Among the corpses they found diverse goods, garments also, and most precious vessels, and they plundered them, so that they could not carry everything, nor could they remove the spoils in three days, so great was the abundance of plunder.
On the fourth day they assembled in the Valley of Blessing: for because they had blessed the Lord there, they called that place the Valley of Blessing unto this present day.
All the men of Judah and the inhabitants of Jerusalem returned, with Jehoshaphat before them, to Jerusalem with great joy, because the Lord had given them joy over their enemies.
They entered Jerusalem with psalteries and harps and trumpets into the house of the Lord.
The fear of the Lord fell upon all the kingdoms of the lands when they heard that the Lord had fought against the enemies of Israel.
The kingdom of Jehoshaphat was at peace, and God gave him rest on every side.

Nevertheless the high places he did not remove — understand this of the high places dedicated to the true God; for he did remove the high places of idols, as has been said. The reason is added: "And the people had not yet directed their heart to the Lord" — that is, perfectly and totally; for they still offered sacrifice to God on the high places, whereas God had commanded that sacrifice be made to Him in the temple of Jerusalem alone.

Written in the words of Jehu son of Hanani, who compiled them in the Books of the Kings of Israel — that is, in the Books of Chronicles or Diaries, in which he recorded the deeds of the kings of Israel. Hence it is clear that Jehu is the author, at least in part, of the book so frequently cited, namely the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel; and that this book is distinct from the present book of Paralipomenon, since it is cited here within it.
Ships that would go to Tarshish — see what has been said elsewhere on this matter.

The prophet Eliezer son of Dodavahu of Mareshah prophesied against Jehoshaphat, saying: "Because you have made a league with Ahaziah, the Lord has broken your works, and the ships were wrecked and could not go to Tarshish."
He killed all his brothers with the sword, and certain of the princes — perhaps because they opposed his new religion and idolatry. If so, they truly died as martyrs.
A lamp — that is, the succession of a royal and illustrious lineage. See Psalm 131:17.

Nevertheless Edom rebelled — Idumea threw off the yoke of the impious King Joram of Judah; and thus was fulfilled the prophecy of Jacob in Genesis 27:40, foretelling that Esau would shake off the yoke of Jacob — meaning that the Idumeans would throw off the yoke of the Jews.

At that time Libnah also revolted — Libnah was an ancient city in the tribe of Judah. Not only the foreign Idumeans, but even the subject inhabitants of Libnah rebelled against the impious Joram, though he was their king. As Adrichomius notes in his description of the tribe of Judah (no. 176): Libnah, also called Lobna and Labana, was a strong and fortified Levitical city, not far from Lachish, which in ancient times had its own king; after he was slain, Joshua also conquered it. When the Jewish state came under monarchy, Libnah defected from Joram son of Jehoshaphat on account of the king's impiety; it was later besieged by Sennacherib king of Assyria. It is now a village in the region of Eleutheropolis called Lobna, about which Isaiah writes, according to Jerome. Some place it in the tribe of Dan.

There came a letter to him from Elijah the prophet — One asks: what kind of letter was this, when was it written, and how was it sent by Elijah? Note that Elijah had long since been taken up from the earth in a fiery chariot into heaven. For in the time of Jehoshaphat, father of this Joram, it was Elisha who was flourishing and prophesying, having succeeded the already-raptured Elijah, as is clear from 2 Kings 3:11. Cajetan's view that this is a different Elijah, distinct from the Tishbite, has no appearance of probability and is stated as a novelty without foundation. Similarly groundless is what Vatablus says — that Elisha is meant here by "Elijah" because the spirit of Elijah had settled upon him. Others, like Emmanuel Sa, think Elijah wrote this letter before his rapture and gave it to Elisha or someone similar, to be delivered to King Joram after his taking up. But others generally hold that Elijah sent this letter from paradise — or from whatever place he has dwelt since his rapture together with Enoch — either through an angel clothed in human form, or through Elisha or another prophet or holy man.

Lapide addresses the question of how Elijah could have written a letter to Jehoram, since Elijah had already been taken up to heaven. He notes that just as Elijah appeared to Peter, James, and John at the Transfiguration of Christ together with Moses, so here Elijah appeared to some man and gave him a letter written by himself to be delivered to Jehoram. This is the view of the Hebrews in Seder Olam ch. 17, and is followed by Lyra, Serarius, Sanchez, Bellarmine, and Salian, who hold that the letter was given by Elijah nine years after his rapture — for he was taken up in the 19th year of Jehoshaphat, who then reigned six more years until his 25th year, and the letter was given in the third or fourth year of Jehoram's sole reign after his father Jehoshaphat's death. Elijah wrote from paradise in order to strike greater fear into the impious Jehoram and move him to conversion, and also to show how great a care the saints, even after this life, have for the faithful — a point also held by Genebrard (Chronol. bk. 1), Joseph Acosta (bk. 3), and others. # GEM

Elijah's letter severely rebukes Joram for having departed from God and from the religion of his father Jehoshaphat, and for having slain his brothers in a manner akin to parricide. Elijah threatens him with three plagues: first, a public plundering of the goods of the king, the kingdom, and the people, including the seizure of his wives and children, which the Philistines, Arabs, and Ethiopians soon carried out (v. 16); second, a grave and prolonged sickness lasting two years, so that his bowels would fall out and he would breathe out his wretched soul in pain and stench (v. 18); third, that he would be denied the honor of a royal burial. This third punishment Elijah does not explicitly state, but it followed from the two prior punishments and from his infamous life and death, as is clear from v. 19.

Except Jehoahaz — This is the one called by the inverted name Ahaziah. For he alone survived and succeeded his father Joram in the kingdom, as is evident from the following chapter, v. 1. So says Bede.

And the people made no burning for him, like the burning of his fathers — The burning was not of the king's corpse (for that was buried in the sepulchre of David and Solomon, or in his own magnificently erected mausoleum), but rather of aromatic spices and precious ointments beside the corpse — a ceremony performed with great pomp and ambition in honor of the royal majesty.

Lapide discusses the chronological problem of Ochozias (Ahaziah) being said to be 42 years old when he began to reign. Genebrardus and Serarius respond that these 42 years are to be computed from the sixth year of Omri (Amri), who was the maternal grandfather of Ahaziah, whose idolatrous ways Ahaziah followed. For Omri began to reign alone in Samaria — which he founded and made the metropolis of the kingdom of Israel — in the sixth year of his reign, and reigned there eight years; his son Ahab succeeded him and reigned 22 years along with his son Ahaziah; Joram likewise his son reigned 12 years; and in Joram's twelfth and final year, this Ahaziah son of Joram began to reign in Judah, and in that same year was slain by Jehu along with Joram king of Israel. Adding 8 years of Omri, 22 of Ahab, and 12 of Joram yields the 42 years in question. It seems the Israelites reckoned their era from the founding of Samaria, counting years from it as historians count from the founding of Rome. Similarly Absalom is said to have rebelled against David after 40 years, reckoned from David's first anointing as king (2 Sam. 15:7). However, this is objected to, because these 42 years are said to belong to Ahaziah, not to the kingdom of Samaria; indeed the Hebrew text plainly reads: "Ahaziah was 42 years old when he began to reign." Moreover, Ahaziah was king of Judah — why then would Scripture compute his years from the separate, indeed hostile, kingdom of Samaria?

Lapide presents a second solution proposed by St. Jerome in his Traditions, the Glossa, Lyra, Hugo, Dionysius, Gordonus, Mendoza, Vatablus, and Francisco Suarez (Disp. 6, sect. 1): that Joram began to reign at age 32, co-reigning with his father Jehoshaphat for twenty years, and then reigning alone for eight years after Jehoshaphat's death — only these eight being counted for chronological purposes — so that he died not at age 40 as Josephus says, but at 60; thus Ahaziah his son, who succeeded him, was 42 years old, although 2 Kings 8:26 says he was 22, because he had co-reigned with his father Joram for 20 years, being 22 when he began co-reigning, and 42 when he reigned alone after his father's death.
