However, Lapide objects that this would require Jehoshaphat to have fathered Joram at age eight, which is impossible: Jehoshaphat began to reign at age 35 and reigned 25 years dying at 60; adding the years Joram reigned after him, and subtracting 42 years for Ahaziah and approximately 18 for Joram when Ahaziah was born, only eight years remain for Jehoshaphat at Joram's birth — an impossibility. Lapide also notes that this solution requires three kings — grandfather Jehoshaphat, son Joram, and grandson Ahaziah — to have reigned simultaneously for 20 years, which is novel and unheard of.

Lapide presents a third solution offered by Sanchez and others: that Joram co-reigned with his father Jehoshaphat for eight years, then reigned alone for twenty years; but because he was struck by God with an incurable disease (2 Chr. 21:19), Ahaziah his son administered the kingdom in his father's sick and wasting condition, so that these twenty years are included in the 42 years of Ahaziah. Thus Ahaziah was 22 when he began co-reigning with his father Joram, and 42 when he reigned alone after his father died twenty years later. Lapide raises three objections: first, that Joram suffered from this disease for only two years, as stated in 2 Chr. 21:19; second, that if this were so, those twenty years should have been reckoned as years of Ahaziah's reign, not merely of his life, so that the continuous chronology of the kings of Judah would be preserved — otherwise these twenty years are missing from the royal chronology, and indeed no chronologer counts them, which is a sign they did not exist; third and most importantly, the years of Joram king of Judah and his son Ahaziah are subsumed within the years of Joram king of Israel, as is evident from 2 Kings.

Lapide notes a chronological point: Joram king of Israel began to reign in the 18th year of Jehoshaphat and reigned twelve years total (cf. 2 Kings 3:1), meaning he reigned seven years after Jehoshaphat's death, since Jehoshaphat himself reigned 25 years. Both Joram king of Israel and Joram king of Judah, together with Joram's son Ahaziah, died in the seventh year after Jehoshaphat's death. Therefore none of these kings could have reigned twenty years after the death of Jehoshaphat.

In the seventh year, Joiada was strengthened. This Joiada was a high priest of great spirit and virtue, who restored the royal line to the kingdom, and lived 130 years (2 Chr. 24:15). Some, including our Henry Samerius in his chronology, think he was not the high priest but a chief priest of the tribe of Judah — namely, one of those dispersed throughout the tribe of Judah who had care of it. Their reason is that in the catalogue of high priests found in 1 Chr. 6, 1 Ezra 7:1, and in Josephus book 10, his name does not appear among the high priests.

The Levites and heads of families were gathered from all the cities of Judah and came to Jerusalem, so that the covenant for restoring the king might be made with broad and representative support from all Israel.

The entire assembly made a covenant in the house of God with the king. Joiada declared: "Behold, the son of the king shall reign, as the Lord has spoken concerning the sons of David" — affirming the Davidic covenant as the basis for Joash's legitimacy.
Joiada then laid out the specific plan of action, assigning roles to the priests, Levites, and gatekeepers to secure the temple and protect the young king during the coronation.

A third of the priests and Levites coming on the Sabbath were to guard the gates, a third the king's house, and a third the Foundation Gate, while the rest of the people remained in the courts of the Lord's house — each station carefully assigned to prevent any interference.

No one except the priests and ministering Levites was to enter the house of the Lord, for they alone were sanctified for that service; the rest of the people were to keep the Lord's watch outside, maintaining order and security.

The Levites were to surround the king, each man armed with his weapons; anyone else entering the temple was to be killed. They were to remain with the king both when he entered and when he went out, providing a complete armed guard.

The Levites and all Judah did exactly as Joiada the priest commanded. Each captain took his men — those coming on Sabbath duty together with those whose Sabbath duty was ending — for Joiada had not dismissed the weekly rotating companies, retaining all divisions simultaneously for this occasion.

Joiada the priest gave to the captains the spears, shields, and bucklers of King David which had been consecrated and kept in the house of the Lord, arming the guard with these sacred weapons for the protection of the rightful king.
He stationed all the people, each holding his weapon, from the right side of the temple to the left side, around the altar and the temple, forming a complete armed perimeter around the king.

They brought out the king's son, placed the crown upon him, and gave him the testimony (that is, the book of the Law) to hold in his hand, and made him king. Joiada the priest and his sons anointed him, and all cried out: "Long live the king!" — the full rite of coronation including both the diadem and the Law, signifying that the king rules under God's word.

When Athaliah heard the noise of the people running and praising the king, she came into the temple of the Lord to see what was happening — drawn by the tumult into the very place that would witness her downfall.

She saw the king standing on the steps at the entrance, with the princes and trumpeters beside him, and all the people of the land rejoicing and sounding trumpets and playing instruments, and the singers leading praise. She tore her garments and cried: "Treason! Treason!" — recognizing that her usurped power had been overthrown.

Joiada the priest went out to the captains and commanders of the army and ordered them: "Bring her outside the precincts of the temple and let her be killed with the sword." He commanded that she not be killed in the house of the Lord, out of reverence for the sacred place.

They seized her and led her away, and when she came to the Horse Gate of the king's palace, they killed her there — thus the usurping queen met her end, slain by the sword outside the holy precincts as the priest had commanded.

Joiada then made a covenant between himself, all the people, and the king, that they would be the people of the Lord — renewing the bond of the covenant with God as the foundation of the restored Davidic monarchy.

All the people went to the house of Baal and tore it down, breaking its altars and images in pieces, and killed Mathan the priest of Baal before the altars — the restoration of true worship requiring the complete destruction of idolatry.

Joiada appointed overseers for the house of the Lord under the authority of the priests and Levites, whom David had assigned to offer burnt offerings to the Lord as written in the Law of Moses, with joy and singing according to the arrangement of David — restoring worship to its divinely ordained order.
He also appointed gatekeepers at the gates of the house of the Lord so that no one unclean in any matter could enter — maintaining the holiness of the sanctuary as the Law required.

Joiada took the captains, the mighty men, the rulers of the people, and all the people of the land, and they brought the king down from the house of the Lord and led him through the upper gate into the king's palace, and set him on the royal throne — the legitimate king restored to his rightful seat.

All the people of the land rejoiced and the city was quiet, for Athaliah had been slain with the sword. Thus peace and order were restored with the death of the tyrant and the establishment of the rightful king.

Lapide notes that the Hebrew Chronicle (chronicon Hebraeum) places Jehoiada among the High Priests. Josephus likewise calls him archiereus (ἀρχιερεύς), and Scripture calls him both Pontiff and Prince of the Priesthood (princeps Sacerdotum), that is, Supreme Pontiff. The reasoning is reinforced by the fact that Jehoiada himself restored King Joash to the kingdom and anointed him king — which was the function of the Supreme Pontiff. Furthermore, the commands and orders he issues here to all the priests, Levites, and the whole people regarding the guarding of the king and the temple prove that he was the supreme pontiff over all.

Neither Josephus nor Scripture (1 Chr. 6 and 1 Ezra 7:1) enumerates all the High Priests, but omits some. For from King Jehoshaphat to Josiah — a span of 238 years — only four High Priests are counted: Ahitub, Meraioth, Zadok, and Shallum; each of whom would have had to serve in the pontificate for 59 years, which is incredible. Therefore Jehoiada and several others were omitted, because they were not of the line of Eleazar, the firstborn son of Aaron, to whose descendants the pontificate belonged by right of primogeniture.

It is possible that Jehoiada, like many others, had a different name. Hence some scholars think that the one placed among the Pontiffs by Josephus under the name Sedeas is Jehoiada, and that the following figure called Julas is Zechariah the son of Jehoiada. For Abulensis, Torniellus, and others hold that Zechariah succeeded his father Jehoiada in the pontificate, though the Hebrew Chronicle disagrees.
Joas began to reign at seven years old and reigned forty years in Jerusalem. His mother's name was Sebia from Beersheba.

Joas did what was good before the Lord all the days of the priest Joiada. Joiada restored religion and the kingdom to Judah equally, and therefore after his death was buried by King Joas in the sepulchre of the kings. The Hebrew Chronicle gives Joiada this epitaph and eulogy: "Joiada grew old, full of days, and died when he was 130 years old." Indeed, when the corruption (of idolatry) and ruin arose in the days of Solomon, Joiada was born at the same time to correct it. For Joiada was born in approximately the 35th year of Solomon's reign, and died in the 28th year of King Joas of Judah. So Salianus. # GEM
Joiada took two wives for him, from whom he begot sons and daughters.

He gathered the priests and Levites and commanded them to go out to the cities of Judah and collect money from all Israel for the repairs of the temple annually, and to do this with haste. But the Levites acted with some negligence. Regarding "who come at the Sabbath": so that they might begin their weekly turn of service in the temple on the Sabbath; for the Jews began their week from the Sabbath, just as we begin from Sunday, and ended it on the same day when it came around again.

The remaining multitude (both of Levites and of the faithful people) shall observe the watches of the Lord — that is, shall perform their office in the court of the house of the Lord, i.e., of the temple — so that if any tumult should arise from the soldiers whom Athalia might send to kill Joas, they themselves might stop, hinder, and repel them. For this reason Joiada had commanded in verse 5 that the remaining multitude should stand in the courts of the house of the Lord — namely in the court which was like the temple of the laity and was divided into various lesser courts. For Joiada wished the newly crowned boy-king to be protected not only by the guard of the Levites but also by the faithful people, lest any violence or treachery be done to him by his impious grandmother Athalia.

The upper gate. This was the gate of the temple which led to the king's palace. It is called "upper" because the royal palace was lower, and from it, situated in a lower place, one ascended through this gate into the temple, as is clear from 4 Kings 11:6 and 19. The same gate in verse 5 is called the "gate of the foundation," because it was erected in the very foundations of the temple, that is, in the Mello; so Villalpando, p. 219. This gate above all others was ordered to be guarded, because through it Athalia would come from the palace into the temple with her soldiers and attendants.
Athaliah, most impious, and her sons destroyed the house of God and adorned the shrine of Baal with all things that had been consecrated in the temple of the Lord.
The king therefore commanded and they made a chest, placing it beside the gate of the house of the Lord on the outside.
It was proclaimed throughout Judah and Jerusalem that each person should bring to the Lord the tribute which Moses, servant of God, had established for all Israel in the desert.
All the princes and all the people rejoiced and entered and contributed into the chest of the Lord, filling it completely.

Whenever the time came to bring the chest before the king through the hands of the Levites (for they saw that there was much money), the king's scribe and the one appointed by the high priest would come and empty out the money that was in the chest; and then the chest was carried back to its place; and thus they did every single day, and an immense amount of money was gathered.

This money the king and Jehoiada gave to those who oversaw the works of the house of the Lord; and they hired from it stone-cutters and craftsmen skilled in each kind of work to restore the house of the Lord, as well as smiths in iron and bronze, so that what had begun to fall might be supported.
Those who did the work labored diligently, and the scarring of the walls was covered over by their hands; they raised up the house of the Lord to its former state and made it stand firm.

When all the works were completed, they brought the remainder of the money before the king and Jehoiada; from it were made vessels for the temple's ministry and for burnt offerings, also bowls and other vessels of gold and silver; and burnt offerings were continually offered in the house of the Lord all the days of Jehoiada.
Jehoiada grew old, full of days, and died when he was one hundred and thirty years old.
They buried him in the City of David with the kings, because he had done good with Israel and with his house.
But after Jehoiada died, the princes of Judah came in and prostrated themselves before the king; and he, won over by their flattery, yielded to them.
They abandoned the temple of the Lord God of their fathers and served the sacred groves and idols, and wrath came upon Judah and Jerusalem on account of this sin.
God sent prophets to them to bring them back to the Lord; these testified against them, but they refused to listen.

The Spirit of God clothed Zechariah the son of Jehoiada the priest, and he stood before the people and said to them: Thus says the Lord God — Why do you transgress the commandment of the Lord, which will not profit you, and have forsaken the Lord so that he may forsake you?
They conspired against him and, at the king's command, stoned him in the court of the house of the Lord.

King Joash did not remember the kindness which Jehoiada his father had shown him, but killed his son. As he was dying, Zechariah said: May the Lord see and require it. Lapide explains: "May he see" — that is, may God behold Zechariah's innocence and uprightness, by which, acting from his duty as Pontiff, he reproved the king for worshipping idols; and may he likewise behold the king's impiety and ingratitude, by which he ungratefully, unjustly, and impiously kills the son of Jehoiada the Pontiff, his own uncle to whom he owes his life and kingdom. "And require" — that he may punish and avenge his death and such great crimes by just judgment. Zechariah said this from a zeal for justice, which was the spirit of Elijah and of the Old Testament. But in the New, Christ and the Apostles slain by the Jews prayed to God not for vengeance but for pardon upon their killers. Hence Christ on the cross: "Father, forgive them, for they know not what they do" (Matt. 27), and St. Stephen when being stoned, like this Zechariah: "Lord, do not hold this sin against them" (Acts 7). # GEM
When the year had come around, the army of Syria came up against him and entered Judah and Jerusalem, killing all the princes of the people and sending all their plunder to the king in Damascus.

Although the Syrians came in a very small number, the Lord delivered into their hands an immense multitude, because they had forsaken the Lord God of their fathers; and upon Joash also they exercised shameful judgments. Lapide notes that God heard the prayer of Zechariah and punished Joash and the Jews in multiple ways: first, in the very next year Hazael king of Syria with few troops slew an immense multitude of Jews and carried off the treasures of the temple and the royal palace into Syria; second, upon Joash the king they exercised shameful judgments (v. 24), reproaching him for his ingratitude and perfidy, afflicting him with various reproaches, indignities, and apparently beatings, as Nebuchadnezzar afflicted the treacherous Zedekiah (2 Kgs. 25).

When the Syrians departed they left him with great infirmities; his servants rose up against him in vengeance for the blood of the son of Jehoiada the priest, and killed him in his bed, and he died; and they buried him in the City of David, but not in the sepulchres of the kings. Lapide notes this is the third punishment: from these reproaches and wounds, and from a fresh chastisement from God, Joash fell into a grave illness that brought him great pain and weakness; fourth, his servants conspired against him and killed him as he lay in bed in the house of Millo, which was near the palace. Josephus adds that these conspirators were friends of Zechariah.
Those who plotted against him were Zabad son of Shimeath the Ammonitess, and Jozabad son of Shimrith the Moabitess.

His sons, and the great sum of money that had been collected under him, and the restoration of the house of God, are written more carefully in the Book of Kings. And Amaziah his son reigned in his place. Lapide notes that this chapter has been expounded at 2 Kgs. 12.
Amaziah was twenty-five years old when he began to reign, and reigned twenty-nine years in Jerusalem. His mother's name was Joadan of Jerusalem.
He did what was right in the sight of the Lord, yet not with a perfect heart — indicating that his virtue was incomplete and mixed with self-interest, as the subsequent narrative confirms.

When he had strengthened his hold on the kingdom, he executed the servants who had murdered his father King Joash, showing both prudence in waiting until secure, and justice in punishing the regicides.

Yet he did not put their children to death, as it is written in the Law of Moses: "Fathers shall not be put to death for their children, nor children for their fathers, but each shall die for his own sin" (Deut. 24:16). Lapide notes this as a remarkable example of Amaziah following the Mosaic law precisely, restraining vengeance within its proper limits.

Amaziah mustered Judah and organized them by families, appointing commanders of thousands and hundreds over all Judah and Benjamin. He numbered those twenty years old and above, and found three hundred thousand choice men fit for war, able to handle spear and shield.
He also hired one hundred thousand mighty warriors from Israel for one hundred talents of silver — indicating his intention to supplement his own forces with mercenaries from the northern kingdom.

But a man of God came to him and said: "O king, do not let the army of Israel go with you, for the Lord is not with Israel — with all the children of Ephraim." God here forbids alliance with the schismatic northern kingdom, whose sin separates them from divine favor.

But if you suppose that strength of army makes for victory in war, God will make you fall before the enemy; for it is God's power to help or to cast down. Lapide emphasizes that victory belongs to God alone and not to numbers or human force, a perennial truth of sacred history.

Amaziah said to the man of God: "But what shall we do about the hundred talents which I have given to the troops of Israel?" The man of God answered: "The Lord is able to give you much more than this." Lapide notes the prophet's reply teaches that no financial loss should deter obedience to God, who can restore far more than is sacrificed.
So Amaziah dismissed the army that had come to him from Ephraim, sending them home. They were very angry with Judah and returned home in fierce anger — foreshadowing the harm they would do in v. 13.
Then Amaziah took courage and led his people to the Valley of Salt, where he struck down ten thousand men of Seir (the Edomites), demonstrating that God blessed his obedience by granting victory.

The men of Judah also captured ten thousand alive, brought them to the top of a cliff, and threw them down so that they all were dashed to pieces. Lapide notes this act of cruelty, while not explicitly condemned in the text, stands in contrast to the mercy Amaziah had shown earlier toward his father's murderers' children.

But the soldiers of the army which Amaziah had dismissed, not allowing them to go with him to battle, raided the cities of Judah from Samaria to Beth-horon, killed three thousand people, and took much plunder — divine providence permitting this as a partial punishment for Amaziah's later infidelity.

After Amaziah returned from striking down the Edomites, he brought the gods of the men of Seir and set them up as his gods, bowing down before them and burning incense to them. Lapide marvels at this extraordinary folly: Amaziah worships the gods of a people he had just defeated, gods who could not even protect their own worshippers. # GEM

Therefore the Lord was angry with Amaziah and sent a prophet to him, who said: "Why have you sought the gods of a people who did not deliver their own people from your hand?" — a devastating logical argument against idolatry: these gods failed to save their own devotees, yet you worship them?

When the prophet was still speaking, Amaziah said: "Have we made you a royal counselor? Stop! Why should you be struck down?" The prophet stopped but said: "I know that God has determined to destroy you, because you have done this and have not listened to my counsel." Lapide notes that the king's silencing of the prophet sealed his own doom — rejecting God's mercy offered through the prophetic word. # GEM

Then Amaziah king of Judah took counsel and sent to Joash son of Jehoahaz son of Jehu, king of Israel, saying: "Come, let us look one another in the face." Lapide notes this challenge to battle arose from pride inflated by his victory over Edom — a classic example of how prosperity breeds reckless arrogance.

Joash king of Israel sent back to Amaziah king of Judah, saying: "A thistle on Lebanon sent to a cedar on Lebanon, saying, 'Give your daughter to my son for a wife,' and a wild beast of Lebanon passed by and trampled the thistle." Lapide explains this as a parable of contempt: Amaziah, the thistle, presumes to challenge Joash, the cedar, and will be destroyed by the first passing danger.

The hundred talents of silver: each talent contained six thousand silver shekels, equivalent to three thousand Brabantine florins, each florin being worth four Roman Julii or four Spanish reales. Therefore a hundred talents of silver amounts to three hundred thousand Brabantine florins. If this sum were distributed equally among the hundred thousand hired Israelite soldiers, each soldier would receive three Brabantine florins or twelve Julii — a very great and highly valued sum in that age, when money was scarce and provisions cheap. Some have wrongly supposed these talents to have been of gold, but Scripture expressly states they were of silver.

But there came a man of God: that is, a prophet sent by God. The Hebrew writers in the Seder Olam record that this prophet was Amos, who was the brother of King Amaziah and father of the prophet Isaiah, and they prove this from Isaiah 37:2, where St. Jerome reads "to Elnath son of Amos the prophet" — and so it was once read — though now the Hebrew, Septuagint, and Roman editions consistently read "to Isaiah son of Amos the prophet."

O king, let not the army of Israel go with you, for the LORD is not with Israel: namely because they were His enemies, worshipping idols, that is, the golden calves. Let Christian kings and princes learn here not to seek the help of heretical princes or soldiers, Turks, or other unbelievers; for if they do so, they will have God as their enemy and will feel His vengeance, as Jehoshaphat felt it when God wrecked his ships because they were joined with the ships of the impious Ahaziah king of Israel (ch. 20:37).

Salianus, writing for the year of the world 3208, no. 6, gives this reason: The Lord was unwilling that the impious and faithless should be joined with the faithful. And so, though He might by His wonderful providence favor each separately and grant them victory, when they were united He not infrequently struck them and caused their defeat. Jehoshaphat was with Ahab (1 Kgs. 22) when Ahab was killed and Jehoshaphat himself fell into great peril of his life. He was likewise with Joram when their armies nearly perished of thirst and were almost made prey of the Moabites (2 Kgs. 3). Ahaziah was with Joram at Ramoth-Gilead when Joram was gravely wounded by the Syrians, and presently both kings were slain by Jehu (2 Kgs. 8:28). Now therefore God threatens disaster and ruin to both if they are united, but grants victory to each separately: for Amaziah soon triumphed over the Edomites, Joash over Amaziah, and Jeroboam over the Syrians. God preferred that Amaziah lose his hundred talents of silver — nay, that Judah be plundered by the dismissed Israelites (as appears from v. 13) — rather than admit them to partnership in the war.

Why have you worshipped gods?: Hear Josephus narrating the matter more fully: "The prophet therefore turned in wonder and said that he marveled if the king believed those to be gods who had been unable to bring any help to their own worshippers, who had not protected their devotees from the power of the Hebrews, but had suffered them to be slain and led captive as though the matter did not concern them — nay, that these very gods had been carried off by right of war and had arrived in Jerusalem among the rest of the captive throng. The king, stirred to anger by these words, commanded him to be silent on this matter, threatening punishment if he continued to meddle in others' affairs. Then the prophet said that he would indeed be silent, but that God would not fail to be the avenger of such new wickedness."

You have not heeded my counsel: St. Jerome in his Traditions says that he would have heeded the prophet's counsel if he had burned those idols with fire, torn his garments, scattered ashes upon his head, and given himself wholly to penance. For this was the counsel the prophet had given him; but because he did not comply, the prophet freely and boldly declares to him:

I know that God has determined to destroy you: and indeed He did destroy him, as is plain from v. 27. For the author of the books of Chronicles wrote them to this end — that from the happy outcome of pious kings and the unhappy outcome of impious ones, he might show that the prosperity of kings and kingdoms rests upon true religion and the true worship of God, and that misery consists in its neglect.

The thistle in Lebanon sent to the cedar of Lebanon, saying: "Give your daughter to my son as a wife; but the beasts of the field that are in Lebanon passed by and trampled down the thistle." — This parable is here explained in the commentary on 2 Kings 14.
You say, 'I have struck down Edom,' and your heart has lifted you up in pride. Stay at home; why should you meddle with trouble so that you fall, you and Judah with you?
Amaziah would not listen, for it was of God that he should be delivered into the hand of his enemies, because he had sought after the gods of Edom.
After Amaziah had turned away from the LORD, they made a conspiracy against him in Jerusalem, and he fled to Lachish; but they sent after him to Lachish and killed him there.

Jerome explains this Zechariah as the posthumous son of Zechariah son of Jehoiada, born after his father's death and thus given his father's name. He was called "understanding" on account of his priesthood, and "seeing" on account of his prophecy (Jerome, Traditions). The Hebrews and Greeks read: "understanding in visions of God." Zechariah appears to have had a daughter named Abijah who married King Ahaz and bore Hezekiah — Jerome affirms this as well. No prophetic writing of his survives; the Zechariah among the minor prophets is easily three hundred years later. So Salianus, Cajetan, Dionysius, and others.

Towers in the wilderness refers to rural farmsteads and country houses equipped with towers for guarding crops, fruits, and fields, in which the produce of the fields was stored and flocks sheltered — elsewhere called "castles." Thus Christ traveled through villages and castles, that is, through the farmsteads and hamlets of rustics, preaching the kingdom of God.

Chrysostom (Homily 3 on the Words of Isaiah) expounds these words and notes that they indicate not only Uzziah's pride but also its cause. "When he was strengthened" — or as Chrysostom reads, "when he was powerful" — "his heart was lifted up": so that excessive power is shown to have produced excessive pride. "He could not bear," says Chrysostom, "the magnitude of his rule; but just as from overeating comes bloating, from bloating comes fever, and from fever often death — so here from the eminence of his deeds arose pride, and from pride arose the appetite for things not fitting for him." Just as his father Amaziah, from conquering Edom, grew insolent and foolishly challenged the king of Israel and was thus defeated, captured, and despoiled — so Uzziah his son, swelling from so many victories, presumed to arrogate to himself the priestly office and wished to burn incense to God; but immediately humbled by God, he was driven from the kingdom and the palace, and on account of his wretched leprosy dragged out his life until death. # GEM

Chrysostom (Homily 4 on the Words of Isaiah) exclaims: "See the freedom! See a mind that knows not how to be enslaved! See a tongue that touches the heavens! See incoercible liberty! See a human body and an angelic mind! See a man walking on earth yet dwelling in heaven!" — praising the boldness with which the priests rebuked the king.
Leprosy was the punishment of those who committed sacrilege and who resisted and rebelled against the High Priest and the priests, as Uzziah did here.

Lapide notes that this fragment describes the religious plagues inflicted upon Uzziah and Judah: the earth is violently shaken, the temple is split from above, and a ray of sunlight strikes the king's face — signs of divine judgment upon the king's presumption.

The history written by Isaiah has perished through the injury of time. For in his prophecy Isaiah mentions Uzziah only twice: at chapter 6:1, already cited, and at chapter 14:28, where restraining the Philistines who were exulting at the death of Uzziah their oppressor, he says: "Rejoice not, all Philistia, because the rod of your striker (Uzziah) is broken; for from the root of the serpent shall come forth a basilisk" — namely Hezekiah, great-grandson of Uzziah — who shall torment you all the more sharply, as the bite of a basilisk is sharper than that of a serpent.

Lepers, as in life, so also in death, are separated from the company of others, for leprosy infects and consumes even corpses. This is the fifth punishment of Uzziah for invading the priesthood — that he was buried outside the royal tombs, though near them, whether outside the city or within it, namely in Zion, the city of David.
Joatham was twenty-five years old when he began to reign, and he reigned sixteen years in Jerusalem. His mother's name was Jerusha, daughter of Zadok.

Except that he did not enter the temple of the Lord — that is, to burn incense, as his father Uzziah had done. Joatham imitated his father's piety, but not his rashness in offering incense, which was the office of priests. "And yet the people still sinned" — after their accustomed manner, sacrificing on the high places. This evil was deeply ingrained and common to the people; wherefore Joatham, fearing their offense and rebellion, did not dare to abolish it.
He himself built the upper gate of the house of the Lord and constructed much on the wall of Ophel.

He fought against the king of the children of Ammon and defeated them, and the Ammonites paid him tribute of a hundred talents of silver, ten thousand cors of wheat, and ten thousand cors of barley — in the second and third year.
Joatham grew mighty because he had directed his ways before the Lord his God.
The remaining acts of Joatham, all his wars and deeds, are written in the Book of the Kings of Israel and Judah.
Joatham slept with his fathers and was buried in the city of David, and his son Ahaz reigned in his place — an impious son succeeding a pious father.
Jotham receives an encomium: no virtue was lacking in him — pious and just toward God, and devoted to the public good toward men.

HE BUILT (restored, enlarged, adorned) THE UPPER GATE OF THE HOUSE OF THE LORD, namely the Eastern gate, through which was the entrance to the temple from the front, which was the principal and highest gate.

AND IN THE WALL OF OPHEL: this was a tower in Jerusalem of such great height that one's eyes grew dizzy looking up at its summit. For Ophel in Hebrew signifies darkness or obscurity. See what is said at Isaiah 32. Hear Adrichomius in his Description of Jerusalem, no. 55: Ophel, which Josephus calls Ophlam, was a tower of enormous height, as if thrusting its head into the clouds, and a fortified citadel, encircled by a strong wall, close to the temple, yet outside the wall that enclosed the priests' dwellings. In its wall King Jotham built many structures. And Manasseh later repaired the city walls to this point. The Nethinim dwelt here. The tyrant Menahem, fleeing to this place, was captured and slain. This same tower was burned by the soldiers of Titus.

Achaz was twenty years old when he began to reign, and reigned sixteen years in Jerusalem. He did not do what was right before the Lord as David his father had done. The Chronicler introduces Achaz as the antithesis of David, establishing the theological framework for the disasters that follow: impiety toward God brings punishment through foreign nations.

He walked in the ways of the kings of Israel and moreover cast images for the Baals. À Lapide notes that Achaz not only imitated the schismatic northern kings but surpassed their impiety by adding the worship of Baal, a Phoenician deity, to the sins of Israel.

He burned incense in the valley of Ben-hinnom and passed his sons through fire according to the abominable rites of the nations whom the Lord had driven out before Israel. À Lapide identifies this as the rite of Moloch, whereby children were burned alive as sacrifices — the most heinous of pagan practices, explicitly condemned by the Law — and notes that God had destroyed the Canaanites precisely for this sin.

He sacrificed and burned incense on the high places, on the hills, and under every green tree. À Lapide explains that this triple enumeration — high places, hills, green trees — indicates the universality and shamelessness of Achaz's idolatry, conducted openly throughout the land rather than in secret.

The Lord his God gave him into the hand of the king of Syria, who defeated him and carried away a great multitude captive to Damascus; he was also given into the hand of the king of Israel, who struck him with great slaughter. À Lapide notes the theological principle operative here: God used pagan kings as instruments of divine punishment against an impious Jewish king, demonstrating that He governs all kingdoms and uses even the wicked to chastise the wicked.

Pekah son of Remaliah killed 120,000 in Judah in one day, all valiant men, because they had forsaken the Lord God of their fathers. À Lapide emphasizes the explicit causal connection drawn by Scripture: the magnitude of the slaughter (120,000 warriors in a single day) is directly proportional to the magnitude of the apostasy. This is divine justice made visible in military catastrophe.

At the same time Zichri, a mighty man of Ephraim, killed Maaseiah the king's son, Azrikam the commander of the palace, and Elkanah the second in authority to the king. À Lapide observes that divine judgment fell even upon the royal family and highest officers of state, signifying that no rank or dignity protects the impious from God's wrath.

The men of Israel took captive 200,000 of their kinsmen — women, sons, and daughters — and also took much plunder, bringing it all to Samaria. À Lapide notes the painful irony that Israelites enslaved fellow Israelites, brethren by blood and covenant, and reflects that civil and fratricidal war is among the gravest punishments God sends upon a sinful people.

At that time a prophet of the Lord named Oded met the army returning to Samaria and said: Behold, because the Lord God of your fathers was angry with Judah, He delivered them into your hands, but you have slain them with a rage that has reached up to heaven. À Lapide praises the courage of Oded, who confronted a victorious army flushed with triumph, and notes that the prophet acknowledges God's permission of the victory while simultaneously condemning the excess of cruelty — a distinction between divine permission and human malice that is theologically crucial. # GEM

And now you intend to subjugate the people of Judah and Jerusalem as your slaves — but have you not sins of your own before the Lord your God? À Lapide explains that Oded's argument runs thus: you yourselves are sinners before God; do not add to your guilt by enslaving your brethren, for you will provoke still greater divine wrath against yourselves.

Now hear me and send back the captives you have taken from your brothers, for the fierce wrath of the Lord is upon you. À Lapide notes that the prophet does not merely advise but commands, speaking with divine authority, and that mercy toward captive brethren is here presented as the necessary condition for averting God's judgment on Israel.

Then certain chiefs of the men of Ephraim — Azariah son of Johanan, Berechiah son of Meshillemoth, Jehizkiah son of Shallum, and Amasa son of Hadlai — stood up against those returning from battle. À Lapide commends these four princes who, moved by the prophet's word, placed themselves bodily in opposition to the returning army — an act of great moral courage, since they resisted the will of the victorious soldiers and their commanders.

They said: You shall not bring the captives here, lest we add to our sins and guilt before the Lord; our sin is already great, and the fierce wrath of the Lord is upon Israel. À Lapide observes that these princes show a tender conscience, recognizing that past sins already weigh heavily upon Israel, and that to add the sin of enslaving brethren would be to heap wrath upon wrath — a moral reasoning worthy of praise.

So the armed men left the captives and the spoil before the princes and all the assembly. À Lapide notes the remarkable obedience of the soldiers: moved by the word of four princes acting under prophetic authority, the entire army renounced its plunder — a notable act of collective repentance and restraint extraordinary in military history.

The men designated by name rose and took the captives, and with the spoil they clothed all who were naked, provided them with sandals, gave them food and drink, anointed them, carried all the feeble on donkeys, and brought them to Jericho, the City of Palms, to their kinsmen; then they returned to Samaria. À Lapide dwells on the tenderness and completeness of this act of mercy: clothing, footwear, food, drink, anointing of wounds, and transport for those too weak to walk. He sees in this a prefiguration of the Good Samaritan (Luke 10), and notes that some Fathers cite this passage in that connection. The City of Palms is identified as Jericho. # GEM

At that time King Achaz sent to the king of Assyria for help. À Lapide notes the bitter irony: instead of turning to God in his distress, Achaz sought human assistance from a foreign and pagan power, thereby compounding his sin and setting in motion the very instrument of further punishment.

The Edomites had again invaded and struck down Judah and carried off captives. À Lapide observes that Judah was now attacked simultaneously on multiple fronts — Syria, Israel, Edom, and Philistia — a fourfold chastisement corresponding to the fullness of Achaz's apostasy.

The Philistines also raided the cities of the Shephelah and the Negeb of Judah, and took Beth-shemesh, Aijalon, Gederoth, Soco, Timnah, and Gimzo with their villages, and settled there. À Lapide notes that the Philistines, ancient enemies of Israel, now permanently occupied Israelite territory — a reversal of the conquest under Joshua, signifying how far Judah had fallen from the blessings promised to the faithful.

For the Lord humbled Judah because of Achaz king of Judah, for he had made Judah act sinfully and had been utterly faithless to the Lord. À Lapide stresses the principle enunciated here by the sacred author: a wicked king brings divine punishment upon his whole people. The sins of rulers are visited upon nations, which is why Scripture judges kings so carefully and why good governance is a sacred duty.

Tiglath-pileser king of Assyria came against him and afflicted him instead of strengthening him. À Lapide notes the divine irony: the very king whom Achaz had hired at great expense to be his protector became instead his oppressor — God so disposed events that Achaz's human remedy became an additional punishment, to teach that help apart from God produces only greater misery.

Achaz took from the house of the Lord and the house of the king and of the princes and gave tribute to the king of Assyria, but it did not help him. À Lapide comments that sacrilege — plundering the Temple treasury to pay a foreign king — brought no benefit whatsoever, demonstrating that the robbery of sacred things never profits the robber, and that divine providence frustrated every stratagem of the impious king.

In the time of his distress Achaz became yet more faithless to the Lord — this same King Achaz. À Lapide remarks on the emphatic pleonasm "this same King Achaz," which the sacred author uses to underscore the extraordinary perversity of the man: most sinners repent under affliction, but Achaz, uniquely hardened, increased his contempt for God precisely when suffering should have moved him to conversion. Augustine and others note this as an example of the reprobate heart that adversity hardens rather than softens. # GEM

He sacrificed to the gods of Damascus who had struck him, saying: Because the gods of the kings of Syria helped them, I will sacrifice to them that they may help me. But they were the ruin of him and of all Israel. À Lapide notes the perverse logic of Achaz: he attributed his defeat to the superior power of Syrian gods rather than to the punishment of the true God, and therefore worshipped the very idols of his conquerors — a madness born of idolatrous blindness. The gods who destroyed him he adopted as patrons. This is the ultimate folly of superstition.

Hezekiah commands the Levites: "Hear me, Levites, sanctify yourselves, cleanse the house of the Lord God of your fathers, and remove all uncleanness from the sanctuary." This is the first act of the restored worship, reversing the defilements of Ahaz.
Our fathers have sinned — Hezekiah confesses the sins of his predecessors who abandoned God, turned their faces from the tabernacle of the Lord, and turned their backs upon it.

The fathers closed the doors of the vestibule, extinguished the lamps, ceased burning incense, and offered no burnt offerings in the sanctuary to the God of Israel — a comprehensive abandonment of divine worship under Ahaz.
Because of these sins, the wrath of the Lord was kindled against Judah and Jerusalem, and He delivered them to commotion, desolation, and hissing — as they themselves could see with their own eyes.
The consequence of this impiety: their fathers fell by the sword, their sons and daughters and wives were led captive — all on account of this wickedness.
Hezekiah proposes a covenant with the Lord God of Israel, so that His fierce anger may turn away from them. This is presented as the remedy for all the preceding disasters.

My sons, do not be negligent: the Lord has chosen you to stand before Him, to minister to Him, to worship Him, and to burn incense to Him. Hezekiah exhorts the Levites to fulfill their divinely appointed vocation with diligence.

The Levites rise to obey: Mahath son of Amasai and Joel son of Azariah from the sons of Caath; from the sons of Merari, Kish son of Abdi and Azariah son of Jehallelel; from the sons of Gershom, Joah son of Zimmah and Eden son of Joah — the listing of names signifying the wholehearted institutional response to Hezekiah's reform.

Concerning the sons of Elizaphan: Shimri and Jeiel; of the sons of Asaph: Zechariah and Mattaniah; of the sons of Heman: Jehiel and Shimei; of the sons of Jeduthun: Shemaiah and Uzziel — these Levitical family representatives were gathered for the purification of the Temple.

The Levites gathered their brethren, were sanctified, and entered according to the king's command and the Lord's precept to cleanse the house of God — showing that both royal authority and divine command concurred in this sacred work.

The priests entered the Temple to sanctify it and brought out all the uncleanness they found within into the vestibule, which the Levites then carried out to the brook Kidron — the same Kidron to which unclean things were also cast in the time of other reforming kings.

They began to cleanse on the first day of the first month; on the eighth day they entered the porch of the Temple; they cleansed the Temple for eight days; and on the sixteenth day of the same month they completed what they had begun — the entire work of purification taking sixteen days.
They reported to King Hezekiah: "We have sanctified the entire house of the Lord, the altar of burnt offering with all its vessels, and the table of showbread with all its vessels."

All the vessels which King Ahaz had polluted during his reign, after his transgression, were restored and set before the altar of the Lord — signifying that what impiety had defiled, true repentance and priestly zeal could restore.

Hezekiah rose early in the morning, gathered all the princes of the city, and went up to the house of the Lord — his early rising demonstrating the fervor of his piety and eagerness for divine worship.

They offered seven bulls, seven rams, seven lambs, and seven male goats as a sin offering for the kingdom, for the sanctuary, and for Judah — the number seven being symbolic of completeness and perfection in the expiation offered.
The priests slaughtered the bulls, rams, and lambs, receiving and pouring their blood upon the altar — following precisely the Mosaic ritual prescribed for solemn expiatory sacrifice.

The male goats for the sin offering were brought before the king and the whole assembly, who laid their hands upon them — signifying the transference of the people's sins onto the victims, in accordance with Levitical law.

The priests sacrificed the goats and sprinkled their blood before the altar as an atonement for all Israel; for the king had commanded that the burnt offering and sin offering be made for all Israel — not for Judah alone, showing Hezekiah's concern for the unity of the entire people of God.

Hezekiah stationed the Levites in the house of the Lord with cymbals, psalteries, and harps, according to the arrangement of David, Gad the seer, and Nathan the prophet — for this had been the Lord's commandment through His prophets, showing that liturgical music is of divine, not merely human, institution.
The Levites stood with the instruments of David, and the priests with the trumpets — each order fulfilling its proper liturgical role in the solemn worship.

Hezekiah commanded that the burnt offerings be offered on the altar; and when the burnt offerings began, the song of praise to the Lord began simultaneously, with trumpets and various instruments that David king of Israel had prepared — showing the fitting union of sacrifice and sacred song.

While the whole assembly worshipped, the singers and those who held trumpets performed their office until the burnt offering was completed — the entire congregation united in adoration throughout the sacrifice.
When the offering was finished, the king and all who were with him bowed down and worshipped — showing that adoration follows upon sacrifice as its proper complement.

Hezekiah and the princes commanded the Levites to praise the Lord with the words of David and of Asaph the seer; and they praised Him with great joy and bowed down in worship — the Psalms of David and Asaph being here explicitly designated as the divinely approved liturgical texts for Temple worship.

Hezekiah declared: "You have consecrated yourselves to the Lord; come near and bring sacrifices and thank offerings into the house of the Lord." The whole assembly brought sacrifices, thank offerings, and burnt offerings with a devout heart — showing that the interior disposition of devotion is required for true sacrifice.

The number of burnt offerings brought by the assembly was: seventy bulls, one hundred rams, two hundred lambs — an extraordinary abundance demonstrating the people's rekindled zeal for the Lord after the impiety of Ahaz.
They also consecrated to the Lord six hundred oxen and three thousand sheep — these being the peace offerings, distinct from the burnt offerings already numbered.

The priests were too few and could not skin all the burnt offerings, so the Levites their brethren helped them until the work was completed and the priests had sanctified themselves — for the Levites were consecrated by an easier rite than the priests, and could therefore assist in preparatory tasks.

There were very many burnt offerings, with the fat of the peace offerings and the drink offerings for the burnt offerings; and so the service of the house of the Lord was completed — the full Mosaic ritual being restored in all its elements.

Hezekiah and all the people rejoiced because of what God had prepared for the people, for the thing had come about suddenly — the swiftness of this restoration being recognized as a special grace of divine providence, not the product of long human planning.
Lapide notes that Zechariah, who instructed King Uzziah in the true worship of God, is here mentioned as a key figure in maintaining authentic divine worship during this period.
The "Eastern plaza" (Platea Orientalis) is so called because it faced the eastern gate of the Temple.

To cleanse the house of the Lord refers to the court of the priests, which served as their temple; for it was lawful for no one to enter the Holy Place except a priest, just as only the High Priest could enter the Holy of Holies. Hence here the priests alone are said to have entered the temple and sanctified it — that is, purged it of idols and their superstition — carrying out the filth into the vestibule. The Septuagint reads "into the vestibule," from which the Levites then carried it out and cast it into the brook Kidron.

They began on the first day of the first month (Nisan, i.e., March) to cleanse: meaning that during the first eight days of Nisan they purified the courts and the chambers of the priests and Levites adjoining them, and then during the following eight days they purified the temple itself, that is, the Holy Place.
And for a sin offering — so that a sin-offering victim might be offered according to the rite prescribed in Leviticus 4.

In the words of David and of Asaph the seer — that is, the prophet. He calls the Psalms "words," from which it is clear that they were composed partly by David and partly by Asaph, as are those twelve psalms which bear Asaph's name as their title.
You have consecrated yourselves (literally, "filled your hands") "to the Lord" — that is, you have consecrated yourselves as priests by filling the hand with oil and with sacrificial victims.

Hezekiah sent letters to all Israel and Judah, including Ephraim and Manasseh, inviting them to come to the house of the Lord in Jerusalem to celebrate the Passover. This invitation extended beyond Judah to the ten northern tribes, showing Hezekiah's pastoral concern for all Israel.

By the counsel of the king, the princes, and the whole assembly of Jerusalem, it was decreed that the Passover should be kept in the second month, a provision allowed by the Law (Num. 9:10-11) for those who were unclean or on a journey during the first month.

The reason for the delay to the second month is given: sufficient priests had not yet sanctified themselves, and the people had not yet gathered in Jerusalem. The disruptions caused by previous idolatry had left the priesthood depleted and unprepared.

Messengers were sent throughout all Israel from Beersheba to Dan — the traditional full extent of the land — because many had not kept the Passover as prescribed by law, a reference to the long neglect of proper worship under the idolatrous northern kings.

The couriers carried letters from the king and his princes throughout all Israel and Judah, preaching: "Sons of Israel, return to the Lord God of Abraham, Isaac, and Israel, and He will return to the remnant who have escaped from the hand of the kings of Assyria." This is a formal call to national repentance.

Do not be like your fathers and brothers who forsook the Lord God of their fathers, who gave them over to destruction, as you yourselves see. The recent fate of the northern kingdom under Assyria is held up as a vivid warning against continued apostasy.

Do not stiffen your necks as your fathers did; yield yourselves to the Lord and come to His sanctuary which He has sanctified forever; serve the Lord your God, that His fierce anger may turn away from you. The phrase 'tradite manus Domino' (yield your hands to the Lord) signifies complete submission and dedication to God.

A promise is attached to the call to repentance: if Israel returns to the Lord, their brothers and children held captive will find mercy before their captors and return to this land, for the Lord your God is gracious and merciful and will not turn His face from you if you return to Him. This demonstrates that divine mercy follows genuine repentance. # GEM

The royal couriers traveled swiftly from city to city through Ephraim and Manasseh as far as Zebulun, but the people mocked and ridiculed them. This hardness of heart among the northern tribes explains why so few responded — long apostasy had rendered them contemptuous of true worship.

Nevertheless, some men from Asher, Manasseh, and Zebulun humbled themselves and came to Jerusalem. Even amid widespread scorn, a remnant responded to the call, illustrating that grace moves the hearts of some even when many resist.

In Judah, however, the hand of God worked to give them one heart to do the commandment of the king and the princes, according to the word of the Lord. The unity of Judah in obedience is attributed directly to divine grace, not human merit.
A great multitude assembled in Jerusalem to keep the feast of Unleavened Bread in the second month, showing that the invitation was broadly heeded at least among the people of Judah.

Rising up, the people destroyed the altars that were in Jerusalem and all the altars on which incense was burned to idols, casting them into the Kidron Valley. This removal of idolatrous apparatus was a necessary preparation for authentic worship of the Lord.

The Passover was sacrificed on the fourteenth day of the second month. The priests and Levites, finally sanctified, offered burnt offerings in the house of the Lord. The phrase 'tandem sanctificati' (at last sanctified) implies that their purification had been delayed and was now completed just in time for the celebration.

The priests and Levites stood in their proper order according to the arrangement and law of Moses the man of God. This emphasis on orderly, lawful worship reflects Hezekiah's concern to restore the full Mosaic rite after years of disorder and idolatry.

In the second month. This is commanded in Numbers 9:10, namely that those who were unable to celebrate the Passover in the first month due to uncleanness or some other impediment should celebrate it in the second month.

Priests who could suffice, having been sanctified (that is, consecrated) were not enough. For in order to be priests and to be able to sacrifice, the priests first had to be consecrated by the rite prescribed in Leviticus 8.

At length sanctified. The Hebrew reads: they were filled with shame, because in such great eagerness of the people they had been slow in their own sanctification and purification; whereupon they immediately sanctified and cleansed themselves.

The priests received the blood to be poured out from the hands of the Levites. For all the blood of the victim had to be poured out on the ground by the priest unto God, to signify that He is Lord of the death and life of all. It came from the hands of the Levites, who were then sacrificing by dispensation, because the consecrated priests who were clean were too few to sacrifice so many victims; therefore the Levites assisted them, slaughtered the victims, and gave the flowing blood to the priests so that they might pour it out beside the altar.

Because a great multitude (of priests) had not been sanctified (cleansed from irregularity and legal uncleanness by the purification prescribed by law in Numbers 19); and therefore the Levites immolated the Passover for those who had not come in time to be sanctified before the Lord. The Septuagint renders: who were not able to be purified before the Lord — namely those who, occupied with other things, came too late to be sanctified, that is, to be purged from legal uncleanness. Note: Passover (Phase) signifies (1) the Paschal Lamb sacrificed to God at Passover, and (2) the Paschal victims immolated to God throughout the seven days of Passover, which immolation belonged to priests alone. The immolation of the Paschal Lamb, however, belonged to each father of a household, who in this respect retained the primeval priestly right originally attached to the firstborn and to every father in his own family — provided he was clean; but if he were unclean and polluted, then the Levites immolated the lamb in his place, as is said here and in 1 Esdras 6:10. Those who were unclean could not eat of the Paschal Lamb or other victims; but because this was a public solemnity and a restoration of collapsed religion, and the number of the unclean was great, a dispensation was granted to them equally to eat of them, with Hezekiah interceding to God on their behalf, as follows. By necessity arising from the small number of clean priests, the multitude and devotion of the people gathering from all Israel, and the abundance of victims to be immolated, it appears a dispensation was made with the Levites so that they would sacrifice not only the Paschal Lamb but also other victims. Lapide believes, however, that the priests alone slaughtered the victims — that is, killed and cut their throats in God's honor and poured out the blood, in which the proper nature of sacrifice properly consists and which was easy and required little labor — and then handed the slain victims to the Levites to skin, wash, cut into pieces, arrange properly on the altar, and burn with fire applied beneath. For a single sacrificing priest was not sufficient for all of this, and so he was compelled to use the help and labor of the Levites.

A great part of the people from Ephraim, and Manasseh, and Issachar, and Zebulun, which had not been sanctified, ate the Passover not according to what is written; and Hezekiah prayed for them saying: The Lord who is good will be merciful. The Hebrews, as St. Jerome reports, hold that many of those who ate the Passover while unclean were struck dead by a plague sent from God from heaven; but through Hezekiah's prayer they were spared.

To all who seek the Lord God of their fathers with their whole heart; and will not impute to them that they are not sanctified. A greater internal devotion was required of the unclean who came to the sacred Paschal meal, which God requires and regards more than external lustration — just as now greater purity is required in a celebrating priest than in a communicating layman, especially since the unclean could not be purified so quickly.

And the Lord hearkened to him and was appeased toward the people. God accepted Hezekiah's intercession and pardoned those who, though legally unclean, had eaten the Passover with sincere devotion of heart.

And the children of Israel who were found in Jerusalem kept the feast of unleavened bread seven days with great joy, praising the Lord day by day; the Levites also and the priests, with instruments suited to their office.

And Hezekiah spoke to the heart of all the Levites who had good understanding toward the Lord, and they ate throughout the seven days of the solemnity, offering sacrifices of peace-offerings, and praising the Lord God of their fathers.
And the whole multitude resolved to celebrate also another seven days; which they did with great rejoicing.

For Hezekiah king of Judah had provided for the multitude a thousand bulls and seven thousand sheep; and the princes had given the people a thousand bulls and ten thousand sheep; and a very great number of priests were sanctified. The generosity of both king and princes supplied the means for extending the feast another seven days, and meanwhile so many more priests were consecrated as to suffice for the additional celebration.

And all the assembly of Judah was filled with gladness, both the priests and Levites and all the congregation that came from Israel; the proselytes also from the land of Israel and those dwelling in Judah.
And there was great celebration in Jerusalem, such as had not been in that city since the days of Solomon the son of David king of Israel.

Then the priests and Levites rose up and blessed the people; and their voice was heard, and their prayer came to the holy dwelling of heaven. The priests' benediction was efficacious and God-accepted, ascending to the very sanctuary of heaven — a fitting conclusion to this extraordinary Passover of national restoration.

After the Passover was duly celebrated, all Israel who were found in the cities of Judah went out and broke the idols, cut down the sacred groves, demolished the high places, and destroyed the altars — not only throughout Judah and Benjamin, but also in Ephraim and Manasseh, until they had utterly overthrown them all. Then all the children of Israel returned to their own possessions and cities.

Hezekiah appointed the priestly and Levitical divisions, each man in his own proper office — both priests and Levites — for burnt offerings and peace offerings, to minister and to give thanks and to sing in the gates of the camp of the Lord.

The king's own portion was that from his own substance the burnt offering should be offered morning and evening continually, and also on the Sabbaths, new moons, and other solemn feasts, as it is written in the law of Moses.
Hezekiah also commanded the people dwelling in Jerusalem to give the portions due to the priests and Levites, so that they might be free to devote themselves to the law of the Lord.

When this command spread among the people, the children of Israel brought in great abundance the firstfruits of grain, wine, oil, honey, and of all the produce of the field; and they brought in the tithes of everything in abundance.

The children of Israel and Judah who dwelt in the cities of Judah also brought in the tithes of oxen and sheep, and the tithes of holy things which were consecrated to the Lord their God, and they laid them in heaps, making many great piles.
In the third month they began to lay the foundations of the heaps, and they finished them in the seventh month — indicating how great was the generosity and devotion of the people.
When Hezekiah and his princes came and saw the heaps, they blessed the Lord and blessed the people of Israel for their remarkable liberality and piety.
Hezekiah inquired of the priests and Levites concerning the heaps — that is, why they lay thus piled up — wishing to understand the full extent of the people's offerings.

Azariah the chief priest of the house of Zadok answered him, saying: "Since the people began to bring the firstfruits into the house of the Lord, we have eaten and been satisfied and there remains an abundance, because the Lord has blessed His people; and this great remainder is what you see." The blessing of God multiplied what was offered, as He always repays generosity with superabundance. # GEM
Hezekiah therefore commanded that storerooms be prepared in the house of the Lord. When this was done, they faithfully brought in the firstfruits, tithes, and all that had been vowed.

All the firstfruits, tithes, and votive offerings were brought in faithfully. Conaniah the Levite was the chief overseer of these, and his brother Shimei was second — showing the importance of faithful stewardship over sacred goods.

Under Conaniah and his brother Shimei were appointed: Jehiel, Azaziah, Nahath, Asahel, Jerimoth, Jozabad, Eliel, Ismachiah, Mahath, and Benaiah — all superintendents under the authority of Hezekiah the king and Azariah the high priest of the house of God, to whom all these matters pertained.
Kore the son of Imnah the Levite, keeper of the East Gate, was over the freewill offerings made to God, to distribute the oblations of the Lord and the most holy things.

In the gates of the camps of the Lord means in the gates of the Temple; for in the Temple the divisions of the priests and Levites were arranged in their orders, distributed like military formations, so that they appeared to be camps, battle-lines, and armies of God, as is clear from Numbers 2 and following, where the Levites are said to go out and enter for the service of the Temple. Hence the Church is called "an army set in array of camps" (Cant. 6:9).

But the king's portion was — that is, King Hezekiah took upon himself these duties concerning the ministries of the Temple, so that he himself would supply the expenses for the continual sacrifice and other victims, while the people would support the priests and Levites (whose revenues had been scattered and dissipated by Ahaz and other impious kings), so that they might be free to attend to the law of the Lord — namely, freed from the concern of procuring food, they might devote themselves entirely to their office and the ministry of the Temple.

Tithes of holy things which they had vowed to the Lord: The Hebrew reads "tithes of sanctities" (that is, of holy things) which had been sanctified to God. The meaning is: they offered tithes of all their goods which by law are commanded to be sanctified to God, that is, consecrated and offered (Num. 18:8ff.), and moreover other tithes not prescribed by law but which they themselves had voluntarily sanctified to God, that is, vowed, dedicated, and set apart, as our translator renders it. The sense is: besides the tithes prescribed by law, they added others out of devotion and generosity, which they had dedicated and vowed to God (Num. 18:16).
They began to lay the foundations of the heaps means they began to gather the first heaps; for what comes first serves as the foundation, as it were, of what follows. So Vatablus.

That they might prepare storerooms: The Septuagint reads "pastophoria," that is, side-chambers or rooms, in which, as in granaries, the firstfruits, tithes, and other things offered to the Temple might be stored.

Consecrated in the Holy of Holies — meaning that they are most holy because they have been dedicated to the Temple and to the most holy God; for the firstfruits were not offered in the Holy of Holies, but in the court before the priests. With a similar phrase in verse 6 he called tithes "of holy things," that is, tithes sanctified to God, that is, consecrated and offered.

Excepting males from three years old and upward — that is, besides boys of three years of age, to whom, just as to all others up to the twentieth year, food was provided. The meaning is: not only to the adults who served the Temple, but even to three-year-old boys, and indeed to infants and those lying in cradles; and even to women and little girls, the necessities of life were generously supplied by the overseers, so that the priests and Levites would not be distracted by the care of feeding them, but might devote themselves entirely to God and the Temple. So great was the abundance of things offered, so great the generosity of the offering people, so great the foresight, piety, and devotion of King Hezekiah.

Through the fields — that is, just as Hezekiah made provision for the Levites dwelling in Jerusalem, so also he made provision for grain for those who were dispersed outside Jerusalem through the fields and other cities.
Hezekiah did all these things throughout all Judah, and he worked what was good and right and true before the Lord his God.
In all the service and ministry of the house of the Lord, according to the law and the ceremonies, desiring to seek his God with his whole heart; and he did it and prospered.

After these things and this faithfulness, Sennacherib came — that is, after these things truly done by Hezekiah and truly narrated by me, God, wishing to prove, exercise, and perfect the piety, virtue, and confidence that Hezekiah had in God, permitted Sennacherib to come and invade Judaea.

The stream (the spring of Gihon, as is clear from v. 30) which flowed through the midst of the land — namely, the land adjacent to Jerusalem. That is, Hezekiah partly blocked the waters, and especially the spring of Gihon, so that the camp of Sennacherib would be deprived of them; and partly by cutting through rock diverted them into the cisterns of the city, so that the besieged Jews would have water for drinking and other uses. See what is said at Sirach 48:19.

The Lord sent an Angel. Herodotus in his Euterpe corrupted this history, saying that Sennacherib invaded Egypt but was put to flight by the priest of Vulcan through mice sent in who gnawed the bows and weapons of the Assyrians — for he substitutes Egypt for Judaea, and a priest for Hezekiah.

Lapide notes that Sennacherib was preserved to suffer the gravest possible punishment — just as Pharaoh was kept alive through ten plagues only to perish last. It was fitting that he endure the most grievous loss of his mighty and flourishing army and his immense wealth, a loss he would not have felt had he perished instantly with his men. It was fitting that he suffer incredible disgrace among his own people, through whose fault or incompetence so great a slaughter had struck the most powerful kingdom. Finally, it was fitting that he be consumed by a far more bitter death, tormented in every way — pierced by many sword blows, by his own sons, in a place consecrated to the deity. Lapide adds that there is no doubt that in this slaughter Rabshakeh, as a blasphemer against God and a reviler of Hezekiah, was cut down and more severely tormented by the Angel; and that Hezekiah and the Jews were enriched with the abundant spoils of so many slain Assyrians.

On the sons (Adrammelech and Sharezer) killing Sennacherib with the sword: Rabbi Solomon, followed by Lyra, gives the reason that Sennacherib, wishing to appease the gods whom he held responsible for so great a slaughter, intended to sacrifice these two sons to them; but they, having gotten wind of this, forestalled their father and slew him. Furthermore, Sennacherib, after receiving his defeat in Judea, fled back to his city Nineveh, where he raged against the Jews and killed many, so that Tobias, in burying them, was stripped of all his goods and barely escaped death by flight — and it was forty-five days after that flight that Sennacherib was killed by his sons.

Jerome in his Traditions (Traditiones) gives the same reason concerning Solomon's case. Serarius and Genebrard in his Chronology also report the same from the Hebrew text of Tobias chapter 1. Lyran and Abulensis offer a different reason.

Azariah the son of Oded: The Spirit of God came upon Azariah, who went out to meet Asa. Lapide notes that this prophet is mentioned nowhere else in Scripture, and that his name means 'helper of God,' which befits his mission of strengthening the king after his victory.

The LORD is with you, while ye be with him: Lapide emphasizes this as a foundational principle of divine covenant — God's presence is conditional upon human fidelity. He cites Augustine, who observes that God does not abandon man unless man first abandons God. This mutual relationship is the heart of the prophetic exhortation to Asa. # GEM

Now for a long season Israel hath been without the true God: Lapide interprets this as referring either to the period of the Judges, when idolatry was rampant, or more broadly to any era in which the people forsook the Law. He notes that without a teaching priest and without law, the people perish spiritually, applying this to the necessity of a learned and faithful clergy in every age.

But when they in their trouble did turn unto the LORD God of Israel: Lapide draws a moral lesson here — affliction and tribulation are the instruments by which God recalls wandering souls to Himself. He cites Gregory the Great, who teaches that adversity is often a greater grace than prosperity, since it compels men to seek God whom they had neglected. # GEM

In those times there was no peace: Lapide explains that this describes the anarchic condition of Israel during periods of apostasy, when external wars and internal disorder followed abandonment of God. He connects this to the natural law principle that true civil peace depends upon justice, and justice upon religion.

Be ye strong therefore, and let not your hands be weak: Lapide notes the prophet's encouragement to Asa to persevere in reform, promising that virtuous labor will receive its divine reward. He applies this to all those engaged in the work of spiritual renewal, warning against sloth and discouragement in holy undertakings.

And when Asa heard these words: Lapide observes that Asa responded immediately to the prophetic word with concrete action — removing the abominations, renewing the altar. He commends this as the model of true hearing of Scripture: not merely intellectual assent but prompt obedience and reform.

And he gathered all Judah and Benjamin: Lapide notes that strangers from Ephraim, Manasseh, and Simeon joined Asa because they saw that God was with him. He applies this spiritually: the grace and blessing visibly resting upon a holy ruler or pastor draws souls from far away to the truth. # GEM

So they gathered themselves together at Jerusalem in the third month: Lapide identifies this as corresponding to the feast of Pentecost, and sees a typological significance in the renewal of the covenant occurring at the same season when the Law was given at Sinai and, in the New Testament, when the Holy Spirit was poured out.

And they offered unto the LORD the same time: Lapide notes the generosity of the offering — seven hundred oxen and seven thousand sheep — as an expression of gratitude proportionate to the greatness of the victory God had granted. He cites Chrysostom, who teaches that thanksgiving should be as great as the benefit received.

And they entered into a covenant to seek the LORD God of their fathers: Lapide praises this solemn public covenant as an act of national religion, noting that it bound the whole people collectively to the worship of the true God. He sees here a prefiguration of baptismal vows and the Church's profession of faith.

That whosoever would not seek the LORD God of Israel should be put to death: Lapide defends the severity of this law as just in the theocratic context of the Old Covenant, where apostasy was equivalent to treason against the divinely constituted state. He cites Thomas Aquinas, who distinguishes the coercive means appropriate to the Old Law from the persuasive means proper to the New.

And they sware unto the LORD with a loud voice: Lapide comments on the public and solemn character of this oath — made with shouting, trumpets, and cornets — as befitting the gravity of the covenant. He notes that external solemnity in worship reflects and reinforces internal devotion of heart.

And all Judah rejoiced at the oath: Lapide observes that this joy was the fruit of a whole heart seeking God, and that such spiritual joy is the reward of sincere conversion. He cites Augustine: 'Thou madest us for Thyself, and our heart is restless until it repose in Thee.' # GEM

And also concerning Maachah the mother of Asa the king: Lapide discusses the famous case of Maachah, whom Asa deposed from her position as queen mother because she had made an idol in a grove. He praises Asa's zeal in preferring the honor of God above natural affection for his own mother, citing Jerome as commending this as heroic virtue.

But the high places were not taken away out of Israel: Lapide carefully distinguishes this verse from the earlier praise of Asa — the high places in the territory of Israel (the northern kingdom) were not under Asa's jurisdiction, though the high places in Judah were largely removed. He notes that even good rulers are constrained in their reforms by the limits of their authority.

And he brought into the house of God the things that his father had dedicated: Lapide praises Asa for completing what his father Abijah had vowed, seeing in this filial piety a model of honoring parental intentions in matters of religion. He notes that the dedication of wealth to God's house reflects proper ordering of temporal goods to divine worship.

And there was no more war unto the five and thirtieth year of the reign of Asa: Lapide interprets this long peace as the divine reward for Asa's faithful covenant with God, citing the principle repeatedly affirmed in Chronicles that fidelity to God brings temporal as well as spiritual blessings.

He worshipped all the host of heaven — that is, the Sun, Moon, and other stars, which march in ordered array as soldiers of God, and fight for Him when needed, hurling thunder and lightning against His enemies. So St. Jerome.
And the Lord spoke to him — not directly, but through Isaiah and other prophets. Cf. 4 Kings [2 Kings] 21:10.

Bound in chains they led him to Babylon. Certain Greek codices and the Author of the Incomplete Work (hom. 1) note that in prison Manasseh was given a small portion of bread made from bran, of little weight, and a small measure of water mixed with vinegar, just enough to keep him alive.
He offered burnt offerings (holocausts) upon it.
In the twelfth year he began to cleanse Judah: this means he began to cleanse it; for he completed this cleansing after six years, in the eighteenth year of his reign, as stated in verse 8.

Moreover he burned the bones of the priests upon their altars: namely, so that by the contact and burning of the corpses of such impious men the altars themselves would be defiled, disgraced, and made objects of horror, lest anyone thereafter should offer victims to idols upon them.
Hilkiah the priest found the Book of the Law of the Lord given by Moses: Genebrard in his Chronology judges this to have been a particular copy.
