I have found the Book of the Law in the house of the Lord: Hilkiah declares the discovery and delivers the book to Shaphan the scribe.
The Prayer of Manasseh exists as a pious and worthy penitential prayer, but it does not have the authority of Canonical Scripture, and therefore has been relegated to the end of the Bibles.

Written in the words of Hozai: The Hebrews, according to St. Jerome, judge this to be Isaiah the prophet. The Septuagint takes Hosai not as a proper name but as a common noun, and translates it as "of the seers," that is, of the prophets. So also Vatablus.

Josiah celebrated the Passover in Jerusalem, which was sacrificed on the fourteenth day of the first month. This Passover is described in greater detail here than in 2 Kings 23, and Lapide notes it surpassed all previous Passovers in solemnity and devotion.
Josiah appointed the priests to their offices and exhorted them to minister in the house of the Lord, showing his zeal not only for external ceremony but for the proper ordering of sacred ministry.

Josiah addressed the Levites, who were the teachers of all Israel in matters of sanctification, commanding them to place the ark in the sanctuary of the temple which Solomon, son of David king of Israel, had built — saying they need carry it no more. He now directs them to minister to the Lord their God and to His people Israel. Lapide notes that the ark had perhaps been moved during earlier periods of apostasy or during repairs to the temple, and Josiah now restores it to its proper place permanently.

Josiah commands the Levites to prepare themselves by houses and families according to their divisions, as David king of Israel had commanded and Solomon his son had arranged, indicating that the Davidic organization of the sacred ministry was the standard to be followed.
They were to minister in the sanctuary according to the Levitical families and their companies, each group fulfilling its appointed role in proper order.

Being sanctified, they were to slaughter the Passover lamb, and also to prepare their brethren so that all could act according to the word which the Lord had spoken through Moses. Lapide emphasizes that ritual purity and preparation of the people were both essential duties of the Levites.

Josiah gave to all the people who were present at the feast of the Passover lambs and kids from the flocks and other animals — thirty thousand in number — and three thousand oxen, all from the king's own substance. Lapide remarks on the extraordinary generosity of Josiah, who gave from his own royal wealth for the entire people.

His princes also freely offered what they had vowed, both for the people and for the priests and Levites. Helcias, Zacharias, and Jahiel, the chief officers of the house of the Lord, gave to the priests for the Passover two thousand six hundred animals and three hundred oxen. Lapide notes this voluntary generosity of the princes mirrors and amplifies the king's own gift.

Chonenias, Semeias, Nathanael his brothers, and Hasabias, Jehiel, and Jozabad, princes of the Levites, gave to the other Levites for the celebration of the Passover five thousand animals and five hundred oxen. The total gifts thus assembled were immense, befitting the greatest Passover since Samuel.

The service was prepared and the priests stood in their place, and the Levites in their companies according to the king's command, showing perfect liturgical order and obedience to royal and divine authority.

The Passover was slaughtered and the priests sprinkled the blood with their hands, while the Levites flayed the burnt offerings. Lapide notes the division of labor: sprinkling of blood belonged to the priests, while the Levites assisted with the skinning.

They separated the portions to give them according to the houses and families of each individual, to be offered to the Lord as written in the Book of Moses; and they did likewise with the oxen. Lapide observes that this careful distribution according to Mosaic prescription shows scrupulous fidelity to the Law.

They roasted the Passover lamb over fire according to what is written in the Law; but the peace offerings they boiled in caldrons, pots, and cooking vessels, and distributed them quickly to all the people. Lapide explains that the Passover lamb itself must be roasted by fire per Exodus 12, while other sacrificial meats could be boiled.

Afterwards they prepared for themselves and for the priests; for the priests, the sons of Aaron, were occupied until night with the offering of burnt offerings and the fat portions; therefore the Levites prepared last for themselves and for the Aaronic priests. Lapide notes the admirable charity and order here: the Levites served others before themselves.

The singers, sons of Asaph, stood in their order according to the command of David, Asaph, Heman, and Idithun the king's prophets. The gatekeepers kept watch at each gate so that they did not depart from their ministry even for a moment; therefore their brethren the Levites prepared food for them. Lapide admires that even the doorkeepers were so devoted to duty that others had to bring them food, a model of perseverance in sacred ministry.

Thus all the worship of the Lord was duly completed on that day, so that they made the Passover and offered burnt offerings on the altar of the Lord, according to the command of King Josiah. Lapide notes that ritus completa — the rite fully completed — signifies nothing was omitted or done carelessly.
The children of Israel who were present kept the Passover at that time and the feast of Unleavened Bread for seven days, fulfilling the entire sacred octave as prescribed in the Law.

There had been no Passover like this in Israel since the days of Samuel the Prophet; and none of all the kings of Israel had kept such a Passover as Josiah kept — with the priests, the Levites, all Judah and Israel who were found, and the inhabitants of Jerusalem. Lapide compares this to the Passover of Hezekiah (2 Chr 30) and concludes Josiah's surpassed it in orderliness and legal precision, even if Hezekiah's was greater in the multitude of participants from the northern tribes. # GEM

This Passover was celebrated in the eighteenth year of the reign of Josiah, the same year in which the Book of the Law had been found and the covenant renewed, showing how reform of doctrine and reform of worship went together.

After Josiah had restored the temple, Necho king of Egypt went up to fight at Carchemish on the Euphrates, and Josiah went out to meet him. Lapide explains that Necho was marching to support the Assyrians against Babylon, and Josiah, likely for political reasons allied with Babylon or seeking to protect his kingdom, chose to oppose this passage through his territory.

But Necho sent messengers to him saying: What have I to do with you, O king of Judah? I come not against you today, but I fight against another house, to which God has commanded me to hasten. Cease from opposing God who is with me, lest He destroy you. Lapide raises the question of whether God truly spoke to Necho and concludes that God can use pagan kings as instruments of His will, as He used Cyrus and Nebuchadnezzar; Necho's words were therefore in some sense a true divine warning, though he was not himself a prophet. # GEM

Josiah would not turn back, but prepared for battle against him, and did not heed the words of Necho from the mouth of God; and he went to fight in the plain of Megiddo. Lapide notes the tragic irony: the most pious of kings perished because he did not discern that God was speaking even through a pagan king. This is a warning that divine warnings must be heeded whatever the instrument through which they come.

There Josiah was wounded by archers and said to his servants to take him away, for he was gravely wounded. Lapide notes that Josiah was struck by arrows while fighting, perhaps having disguised himself as a common soldier, paralleling the death of Ahab (1 Kings 22), though Josiah's death was far more lamented.

Lapide notes: "PUT THE ARK IN THE SANCTUARY OF THE TEMPLE." The Hebrews, as recorded by St. Jerome, hold that the ark was removed from the Holy of Holies during the time of Manasseh or Amon, lest it remain in the temple alongside their idols. It was therefore transferred to the house of Shallum, who was the uncle of Jeremiah and husband of the prophetess Huldah (ch. 34, v. 22). Now, with the idols removed by Josiah, the ark was restored to the temple and its proper place. Having done this, Josiah says to the Levites: "You shall carry it no more," meaning the ark was no longer to be taken out of the temple. Henceforth, he says, you shall minister before the ark to the Lord, and likewise to His people Israel, instructing and guiding the people in the law and worship of God.

Lapide notes on the phrase "to make the Passover...three hundred oxen": This makes clear that the Passover offering signifies not only the Paschal lamb but also oxen and other victims that were sacrificed to God during the Passover feast.

Lapide notes: "AND THE PRIESTS SPRINKLED THE BLOOD WITH THEIR HANDS" — upon the altar of burnt offerings, as was prescribed by the law. For all blood was owed to God, and therefore was poured out upon or beside the altar. The altar represented God Himself. "And the Levites flayed the burnt offerings," so that the priests could then place the flesh, cut into pieces, upon the altar, and burn it for God with fire placed beneath.

Lapide notes: "And they separated them" — namely, the burnt offerings already mentioned (as the Hebrew has it) — so that these might be offered wholly to the Lord and entirely consumed by fire. They separated, I say, the burnt offerings from the other victims, in order to distribute them (that is, divide them) among the houses and families of individuals, so that each might offer them to God as peace offerings, and thereafter arrange a sacred feast from the same and hold a banquet. This is clear from what was said in vv. 7 and following.

Lapide notes: "ASAPH AND HEMAN AND JEDUTHUN, PROPHETS OF THE KING" — that is, singers of David. For singers who sang praises and Psalms to God are called prophets. So the Hebrews according to St. Jerome. Yet some of them were true prophets, such as Asaph, who composed and dictated several Psalms by the inspiration of God.
Lapide notes: "I FIGHT AGAINST ANOTHER HOUSE" — namely, against the king of the Assyrians, as is clear from 2 Kings 23:29.

Lapide notes: They transferred him from his chariot into another chariot that followed him in the royal manner, and brought him to Jerusalem, where he died and was buried in the mausoleum of his fathers. All Judah and Jerusalem mourned for him.

Lapide notes: Jeremiah mourned most of all, whose singers and songstresses, to this very day, repeat lamentations over Josiah, and it has obtained as a kind of law in Israel. Behold, it is said to be written in the Lamentations.

Lapide notes: The rest of the acts of Josiah and his merciful deeds, which were commanded by the law of the Lord, and likewise his first and last works, are written in the Book of the Kings of Judah and Israel.
Josephus claims Josiah died in Jerusalem, but the text indicates he was struck down at Megiddo and his body was then transported to Jerusalem to be buried in the royal sepulchre.

The phrase "it became a law in Israel" means that the lamentation of Jeremiah over Josiah's death became so fixed and established among the Israelites that it had the force of law — singers and choristers would use and sing Jeremiah's lamentation whenever they wished to perform a mournful or funereal song. This lamentation is recorded in the Lamentations of Jeremiah; the Chaldean Targum, Josephus, the Hebrews cited by St. Jerome, Lyra, Abulensis, and Hugo all hold that Jeremiah mourns Josiah's death at the end of the Lamentations. However, as Lapide notes elsewhere, it is more accurate to say that Jeremiah's lamentations over Josiah's death were separate from the canonical Lamentations now in Scripture, and these have perished through the injuries of time.
The people of the land took Joachaz son of Josiah and made him king in Jerusalem in place of his father.
Joachaz was twenty-three years old when he began to reign, and he reigned three months in Jerusalem.
The king of Egypt removed him when he came to Jerusalem, and condemned the land to a tribute of one hundred talents of silver and one talent of gold.
The king of Egypt appointed Eliakim, Joachaz's brother, as king over Judah and Jerusalem, and changed his name to Joakim; Joachaz himself he took away with him and led into Egypt.
Joakim was twenty-five years old when he began to reign, and he reigned eleven years in Jerusalem; and he did evil before the Lord his God.
Nebuchadnezzar king of the Chaldeans came up against him and led him bound in chains to Babylon.
Nebuchadnezzar also carried off the vessels of the Lord and placed them in his own temple.
The rest of the acts of Joakim, and his abominations, and what was found in him, are contained in the Book of the Kings of Judah and Israel; and his son Joachin reigned in his place.
Joachin was eight years old when he began to reign, and he reigned three months and ten days in Jerusalem, and did evil in the sight of the Lord.

When the year had come around, Nebuchadnezzar sent and had him brought to Babylon, along with the most precious vessels of the house of the Lord; and he appointed Sedecias, Joachin's uncle, king over Judah and Jerusalem.
Sedecias was twenty-one years old when he began to reign, and he reigned eleven years in Jerusalem.
He did evil in the eyes of the Lord his God, and did not humble himself before the face of Jeremiah the Prophet, who spoke to him from the mouth of the Lord.
He also revolted from king Nebuchadnezzar, who had made him swear by God; and he hardened his neck and his heart so as not to return to the Lord God of Israel.

Moreover all the chief priests and the people transgressed iniquitously according to all the abominations of the Gentiles, and polluted the house of the Lord which He had sanctified for Himself in Jerusalem.
The Lord God of their fathers sent word to them through His messengers, rising early and warning them daily, because He had compassion on His people and on His dwelling place.
But they mocked the messengers of God, despised His words, and ridiculed His prophets, until the wrath of the Lord rose against His people and there was no remedy.

He therefore brought upon them the king of the Chaldeans, who slew their young men with the sword in the house of their sanctuary; He had no mercy on young man or virgin, nor on the aged or the decrepit, but delivered them all into his hands.
All the vessels of the house of the Lord, both great and small, and the treasures of the temple and of the king and of the princes, he carried away to Babylon.
The enemies burned the house of God, broke down the wall of Jerusalem, burned all its towers, and demolished whatever was precious within it.
Whoever had escaped the sword was led away to Babylon and served the king and his sons until the king of the Persians came to power.

This was to fulfill the word of the Lord spoken through Jeremiah, and that the land might celebrate its sabbaths; for all the days of its desolation it kept sabbath, until seventy years were completed.

In the first year of Cyrus king of Persia, that the word of the Lord spoken through Jeremiah might be fulfilled, the Lord stirred up the spirit of Cyrus king of Persia, who caused it to be proclaimed throughout all his kingdom, and also in writing.

Thus says Cyrus king of Persia: All the kingdoms of the earth the Lord God of heaven has given to me, and He has charged me to build Him a house in Jerusalem which is in Judah. Whoever is among you of all His people, the Lord his God be with him, and let him go up.

In the first year of Cyrus king of the Persians — that is, the year in which Cyrus transferred the monarchy from the Chaldeans to the Persians. This first year of Cyrus was not the first year of his Persian kingdom (in which he began to reign in Persia and from which he reigned a full thirty years, as Eusebius, Africanus in their Chronicles, St. Jerome on Zechariah ch. 10, Pererius on Daniel ch. 5, and others hold), but rather it was the first year of his monarchy over the Babylonian kingdom — namely, when, having defeated Baltasar and captured and overthrown Babylon, he transferred the monarchy from the Chaldeans to himself and the Persians. For he could not rescue the Jews from Babylon and from the hands of the Chaldeans unless he had first overcome and conquered them. This is clearly evident from the words of Cyrus in v. 2.

All the kingdoms of the earth the Lord God of heaven has given to me, and he has commanded me to build him a house in Jerusalem which is in Judaea. Cyrus acknowledges the God of Israel as the true God and attributes his universal dominion to Him, declaring himself commissioned to rebuild the Temple — a remarkable confession from a pagan king.

Who is there among you of all his people? May his God be with him, and let him go up to Jerusalem which is in Judaea and build the house of the Lord God of Israel. Cyrus extends a universal invitation to all Jews, invoking the divine blessing upon those who return, recognizing the God who dwells in Jerusalem as the true God.

And all the remnant in all places where they sojourn, let the men of his place help him with silver and gold, and with goods and beasts, besides the freewill offerings for the house of God which is in Jerusalem. Cyrus commands those Jews who remain in Babylon to assist those returning, both with material goods and with voluntary offerings for the Temple — a distinction is made between obligatory assistance and voluntary gifts.

Then rose up the chief of the fathers of Judah and Benjamin, and the priests and the Levites, and all whose spirit God had stirred up, to go up to build the house of the Lord which is in Jerusalem. Not all the Jews returned, but only those whose spirit God moved — showing that the return was a work of divine grace, not merely human impulse, just as the exile had been a work of divine justice.

And all those who were around them strengthened their hands with vessels of silver, with gold, with goods, and with beasts, and with precious things, besides all that was willingly offered. The neighboring peoples and those Jews remaining in Babylon generously supplied the returning exiles, fulfilling the command of Cyrus and echoing the manner in which the Israelites despoiled the Egyptians at the Exodus.

Also Cyrus the king brought forth the vessels of the house of the Lord, which Nebuchadnezzar had taken from Jerusalem and had put them in the house of his gods. Cyrus restores the sacred vessels that Nebuchadnezzar had plundered from the Temple and placed in the temple of his idols — a signal act of piety and restitution, and a fulfillment of prophetic promise.

Even those did Cyrus king of Persia bring forth by the hand of Mithridates the son of Gazabar, and numbered them unto Sheshbazzar the prince of Judah. The sacred vessels are formally inventoried and handed over through proper officials — Mithridates the treasurer and Sheshbazzar (identified by many with Zerubbabel) as prince of Judah — signifying an official and solemn act of restitution.

And this is the number of them: thirty golden basins, a thousand silver basins, twenty-nine knives, thirty golden bowls. The careful enumeration of the sacred vessels demonstrates the completeness of the restitution and the fidelity with which the sacred patrimony of the Temple was preserved and restored.

Four hundred and ten silver bowls of a second sort, and other vessels a thousand. The vessels are catalogued by type and material, attesting to the great wealth of the original Temple treasury and the remarkable providence by which these holy objects survived the Babylonian captivity.

All the vessels of gold and of silver were five thousand and four hundred. All these did Sheshbazzar bring up with them of the captivity that were brought up from Babylon unto Jerusalem. The grand total — five thousand four hundred vessels of gold and silver — is recorded, and Sheshbazzar leads the returning exiles with this sacred treasure, marking the joyful and providential close of the Babylonian captivity and the beginning of restoration, in fulfillment of Jeremiah's prophecy.

Cyrus is said in 1 Esdras (3 Ezra) 6:17 to have written in the first year of his reign ordering the rebuilding of the Temple. Although Darius the Mede, uncle of Cyrus, is said in Daniel 5 to have succeeded to the kingdom after Babylon's capture, Darius, being old, ceded all governance to his nephew Cyrus, a young man of spirit, and dying that same year transferred the entire empire to him. Therefore Cyrus in the very first year of his monarchy — which was the seventieth and final year of the Jewish captivity in Babylon — liberated the Jews from there.

From Herodotus, Justin, Eusebius, Josephus, and others it is noted that Cyrus began to reign over Persia alone at Olympiad 55, from which point thirty years of his universal reign are counted. He gradually extended his empire beyond Persia, finally captured Babylon, overthrew the Chaldean monarchy, and made himself sole monarch. At that point he released the Jewish captivity in Babylon, when the 70 years of captivity prophesied by Jeremiah (chs. 25 and 29) had been completed. Cyrus did this in the 27th year of his Persian reign, and it is therefore most probable that he reigned as monarch for only three more years after the capture of Babylon — as taught by the Seder Olam (the Hebrew Chronicle), by the Hebrews generally, by Lyra on Daniel, Vatablus on Daniel 1, Josephus (Antiquities 11.2), and others. Daniel, summarizing the years of Cyrus through his deeds, does not go beyond the third year.

Xenophon (Cyropaedia, bk. 8) attributes seven years to Cyrus's principate, which leads Bellarmine, Torniellus, Salianus, and Ribera (on Zechariah 1, n. 33) to think Cyrus reigned seven years as monarch. But those books were written by Xenophon not so much for historical accuracy as to fashion in Cyrus the portrait of an ideal prince. Moreover, those seven years could be counted from Cyrus's subjugation of Asia, not of Babylon. Finally, more credence is owed to 1 Esdras 5 (end) than to Xenophon, where it is sufficiently explicitly stated that Cyrus reigned only two years from the beginning of the Temple's rebuilding — which, as is clear from this chapter, occurred immediately after the release of the captivity, namely in the second year of Cyrus's monarchy. Ezra 3:8 explicitly teaches that the Jews began rebuilding the Temple in this second year; and Ezra 4:6 relates that the Cuthaeans impeded the Temple's construction throughout all the days of Cyrus.

Josephus (Antiquities 11.2) teaches still more clearly that the Cuthaeans impeded the rebuilding without Cyrus's knowledge, he being occupied with other affairs and especially with the Massagetan war, in which he also perished. Hence Ezra ch. 3 relates that the Temple's construction, granted by Cyrus in the first year of his monarchy, was suspended for nine years: namely the two remaining years of Cyrus, and the six years during which his son Cambyses reigned, after whom came Darius Hystaspis, who in the second year of his reign resumed the work. Cyrus therefore did not survive seven years as some suppose. For how could Cyrus, so benevolent toward the Jews, have been ignorant of or tolerated for seven years the obstacles the Cuthaeans placed against the Temple's construction? And how would the Jews, especially Daniel, not have informed Cyrus of the whole matter, either personally or through envoys? Finally, Herodotus, Justin, Berosus, and others write that after capturing Babylon Cyrus waged a final war against the Lydians who had revolted, and then fell in battle against the Massagetae — all of which it is credible he accomplished within three years, for he was most warlike and impatient of rest.

On the etymology of the name "Cyrus": First, Strabo (bk. 15) thinks Cyrus took his name from the river Cyrus. Second, Vilalpando (Apparatus, pt. 1, bk. 3, ch. 2, p. 137) holds that Cyrus was named from "Cyno," meaning dog, which is said to have nursed him — so that Cyrus means, as it were, "canine." For as Herodotus relates in the Clio, Astyages, grandfather of Cyrus, handed the infant Cyrus to Harpagus to expose and destroy him, having heard from an oracle that Cyrus would take his kingdom from him. Harpagus entrusted him to Mithridas, Astyages's cowherd, whose wife was called in Greek "Cyno" (κύων), meaning "dog," and in Median "Spaco" (for the Medes call a dog "spaca"). As Cyrus grew he recalled being raised by the cowherd's wife, always praising her, so that "Cyno" was ever on his lips. His parents, seizing on this name so that it might seem to the Persians that he had been divinely preserved, spread the story that Cyrus when exposed had been nursed by a dog — whence the legend arose. Justin likewise records that Cyrus was raised by a dog, yet does not say he was named from "Cyno," and "Cyrus" appears to be a Persian, not a Greek name.

Third, Suidas (s.v. Cyrus) says that in Greek "Cyrus" is κύριος, meaning Lord or King, because in play among youths he obtained royal authority (κῦρος). The Persians called Darius a tradesman on account of the taxes he imposed; Cambyses they called master; Cyrus they called father — for Darius sold everything; Cambyses was fierce and reckless; Cyrus was clement and devoted to the welfare of the people. But this again gives Cyrus a Greek, not a Persian etymology. Fourth, and most accurately, Plutarch (Life of Artaxerxes Mnemon) states: "Cyrus in Persian means the same as 'sun,'" for the Persians worshipped the sun as a deity, and equally revered Cyrus their monarch — whence Jeremiah calls Cyrus the liberator of the Jews, like a sun. # GEM

As to why Cyrus or a king is compared to the sun, Lapide notes he demonstrated this appropriately through eighteen analogies in his commentary on Isaiah 41:1. It is reported that Cyrus in a dream saw the sun standing at his feet, and when he three times tried to grasp it, it fled away; the Magi, consulted about what this portended, answered that it signified Cyrus would reign for thirty years. Thus Cyrus shone among the kings of the nations and among the Persians like the sun — in wisdom, justice, temperance, magnanimity, and liberality — and was, says Xenophon, the exemplar of all royal virtues. Cicero (On Divination, bk. 1, citing Dionysius the Persian) records: when the sun appeared to Cyrus in sleep standing at his feet, he three times vainly tried to grasp it with his hands; as it slipped away revolving, the Magi declared that from the threefold reaching for the sun it was portended that Cyrus would reign thirty years — which came to pass, for he reached his seventieth year, having begun to reign at forty.

Furthermore, "Cyrus" in Hebrew signifies one like Christ, and God promises through him to subject nations to him and to make him divine and powerful. As Isaiah says: "Who say to Cyrus: He is my shepherd, and he shall fulfil all my will; who say to Jerusalem: Thou shalt be built, and to the Temple: Thy foundation shall be laid" (Is. 44:28). And again (Is. 45:1): "Thus says the Lord to my anointed Cyrus, whose right hand I have taken hold of, to subdue nations before him." # GEM

Cyrus declares that God is with whoever wishes to return from Babylon to Jerusalem, calling this divine will a "precept" (præceptum). He urges all Jews desiring to return to their homeland to gather together and travel back as a group, promising them full liberty to return and to rebuild the city and Temple.

Cyrus professes to recognize the God of the Jews as the true God, stating "He is God who is in Jerusalem" — meaning there is no other true God than the God whom the Israelites have worshipped in Jerusalem. Cyrus thus acknowledges that the gods of the Gentiles are either not true gods at all, or are of lesser dignity and power, subject to the God of the Hebrews — whom Plato and the philosophers called "demigods." Xenophon (Cyropaedia, Book 8) affirms that Cyrus also worshipped the gods of the nations, and that as he lay dying he had a dream inviting him to ascend to the gods; accordingly he adored and invoked Jupiter and the Sun, spoke much about the immortality of the soul and his passing to the gods after death, and then expired.

Concerning "all the rest in every place where they dwell, let the men of his place help him with silver and gold": those Jews who had settled in Babylon or other places, taken wives, begotten children, and purchased fields — so that they cannot or will not leave these things to return to Jerusalem — these are to assist those who do wish to return with silver and gold, both for provisions for the long journey and for the restoration of the city and Temple. The phrase "men of his place" means that each person from his own location and from his own resources — from his fields and houses — should contribute what he has. Cyrus thus makes the Jews remaining in Babylon a kind of tributary people obligated to support those returning to Jerusalem. Moreover, all the remaining peoples, not only Jews but also other nations subject to his dominion, and especially the governors of provinces (whom Josephus calls "royal friends"), were likewise to assist those returning, and by virtue of this edict of Cyrus they did in fact help the departing Jews.

On "Except what they voluntarily offer to the temple of God": The word "except" (excepto) is a term of augmentation and confirmation, as if to say: I know that many have already mentally designated and determined certain gifts which they wish to offer to the temple; these I do not forbid, but praise and confirm, and desire them to be offered; but beyond this I wish each one to add further gifts to serve as provisions for the return journey and for the rebuilding of the city and houses, and for other manifold necessities and uses. Some therefore think that Cyrus fixed a price which each person was obliged to contribute, as later did Darius and Artaxerxes, successors of Cyrus; indeed, that Cyrus decreed that the temple should be built from the royal treasury at his own expense, as appears from Ezra 6. Hence for "except what they voluntarily offer," the Hebrew has "with a freewill offering": meaning, along with the offering which they spontaneously offer to the temple, they shall also give other things for provisions, etc. So Vatablus, Pagninus, and others.

On "And the chief fathers of Judah and Benjamin arose": "Princes of the fathers" are the heads of families; for each family had its own head and father; but different families descended from one common ancestor had their own prince and quasi-Patriarch.

On "King Cyrus also brought forth the vessels of the temple of the Lord, which Nebuchadnezzar had taken": These were 5,400 in number, as stated in verse 11; all of which had been sacrilegiously taken by the Chaldeans and dedicated to their idol Bel, but Cyrus restored them to God and the temple.

On "By the hand of Mithridates the son of Gazabas": The word "son" (filii) is not in the Hebrew nor in the Greek. Hence Vatablus, Pagninus, Marinus, Cajetan, and others translate: "By the hand of Mithridates the treasurer"; Josephus: "Through Mithridates, prefect of the royal treasury." For a treasurer is called gazabar, that is, treasurer, as appears from chapter 7:21, where the Vulgate renders it "keepers of the treasury." Unless one says that our translator understood ben (son) implicitly, and that Gazabar was a common name but an office-specific title, just as we commonly call one who presides over a prince's treasury a "treasurer," as if this name belonged to him not by baptism but by office — in which offices sons often succeed their parents. Furthermore, "Mithridates" is composed of Mithri, meaning "great one, dynast, prince," and dath, meaning "of the law," signifying one who bears the law and pronounces justice.

On "And he numbered them to Sheshbazzar the prince of Judah": This refers to Zerubbabel, grandson of King Jeconiah through his father Salathiel. So Josephus, Lyra, Dionysius, Vatablus, Mariana, Tornielus, Salianus, and others. Therefore both the father Salathiel and the grandfather Jeconiah appear to have died in this first year of Cyrus, since henceforth there is no mention of them, but always of Zerubbabel as leader of Judah. Salathiel gave his newborn son the name Zerubbabel, meaning "stranger in Babylon," because he begot him in the Babylonian captivity; but when Evil-Merodach released his grandfather Jeconiah from prison and made him a guest at the royal table, Zerubbabel's name was changed so that he was called Sheshbazzar, meaning "rejoicing in tribulation." So Eusebius, De Praeparatione Evangelica, Book 11, chapters 3 and 4: for "sas" means rejoicing, "ba" means "in," and "shar" means tribulation. Again, Pagninus in his Hebrew names interprets Sheshbazzar as "six in tribulation"; for "ses" means six or fine linen, and the Hebrews record that he was so named because he stood firm and constant through six tribulations, fulfilling that of Job 5:19: "In six troubles he will deliver you, and in the seventh evil shall not touch you."

Note that among the ancient Hebrews, Chaldeans, Persians, Phoenicians, and other Oriental peoples, it was customary to change the names of foreigners and give them native names proper to their own nation into which they were incorporated — names, that is, signifying something definite and appropriate to the person, just as Adam gave things names suited to their natures (Genesis 2:19), so that names were as it were omens signifying some present or future nature, virtue, or property of the thing. Thus in Daniel chapter 1, the Hebrew names Hananiah, Azariah, and Mishael were changed in Babylon by the Chaldeans into the Chaldean Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego. Similarly in Daniel 2:63, the Hebrew Nehemiah was called by the Chaldeans by the Chaldean name Athersata. The same Plato teaches of the Greeks in the Cratylus. Wherefore Eusebius, De Praeparatione Evangelica Book 11, chapters 3 and 4, draws from this an argument for the remarkable wisdom of the Hebrews, in that their sacred names signified something true and real. # GEM

These are the children of the province — that is, these are the Jews who were born or raised in the province of Babylon and emigrated from it to the ancestral homeland, namely Jerusalem. "Children of the province" refers to those born there from immigrant stock or who dwelt there long as inhabitants. Vatablus notes this phrase signifies either birth or long residence. Hence others understand "children" as meaning "inhabitants." So too are used the expressions "children of Zion," "children of the East," "daughters of Jerusalem," meaning inhabitants. By "Province" is understood Babylon, then powerful and dominant, just as today all understand "the City" to mean Rome. Though Bede interprets "Province" here as Judea, to which the Jews returning from Babylon belonged, as children of the Church returning to their mother.

And they returned to Jerusalem — yet in such a way that they remained as servants subject to masters under Cyrus and the Persians, and as tributaries, as is clear from chapters 6 and 13, and 2 Esdras 6:36. Hence the kings of Persia appointed princes over them, such as Zerubbabel here in v. 2, and Ezra and Nehemiah in ch. 7, and 1–2 Esdras ch. 5.
Who came with Zerubbabel — as leader, and with Joshua as High Priest, who is elsewhere called Jesus son of Josedec, as is clear from Zechariah.
Mordecai — Many hold this to be Mordecai the uncle of Esther, who returned to Judea to recover his paternal possessions, and then returned to Persia, where he had determined to fix his seat.

The number of the men of the people of Israel — that is, of those returning from Babylon to Jerusalem, which I now set forth. Israel here is used for Judah and Benjamin; for only the Jews and Benjaminites returning are numbered here. Elsewhere, when Israel is opposed to Judah, it signifies the ten tribes which, making a schism from Judah and Benjamin, created Jeroboam as their king; for those tribes, having been carried away into Assyria and there thoroughly scattered and as it were destroyed, the name and glory of Israel returned and remained in Judah.

Among those who could not demonstrate their genealogy were certain priestly families — the sons of Hobaiah, the sons of Hakkoz, the sons of Barzillai — who were excluded from the priesthood because they could not find their genealogical records and so prove their priestly descent.

These sought their genealogical record but did not find it, and were therefore ejected from the priesthood. This shows the importance of priestly lineage and the strictness with which the restored community maintained the integrity of the sacred office.

And the Tirshatha said to them that they should not eat of the most holy things until there arose a priest learned and perfect — that is, one endowed with the Urim and Thummim, by which doubtful cases of this kind could be determined. The Tirshatha (i.e., the governor, here Zerubbabel) suspended judgment in uncertain cases, awaiting divine clarification through the high-priestly oracle.

The total of the whole assembly was forty-two thousand three hundred and sixty — a number that the commentary notes should be compared with the parallel passage in Nehemiah, where some discrepancies in individual family totals appear, though the grand totals agree, the differences being accounted for by varying methods of enumeration or by scribal variation in transmission.

And certain of the heads of families, when they came to the house of the Lord which is in Jerusalem, offered freely for the house of God to set it up in its place — that is, they made voluntary contributions toward the rebuilding of the Temple. This generosity of the returning leaders mirrors the voluntary offerings made for the first Tabernacle in the time of Moses, and commends the virtue of liberality in sacred causes.

According to their ability they gave to the treasury of the work: of gold sixty-one thousand drams, and of silver five thousand pounds, and one hundred priests' garments. The offerings were proportioned to each person's means — a principle of sacred giving that Augustine and others commend as the model for all Christian almsgiving, that each should give as God has prospered him. # GEM

So the priests and the Levites, and some of the people, and the singers, and the porters, and the Nethinim, dwelt in their cities, and all Israel in their cities. The orderly resettlement of each class — priests, Levites, singers, doorkeepers, and Nethinim — in their own cities shows the careful restoration of the sacred order of the Mosaic institution, each group returning to its proper function and place in the life of the renewed community.

On the twelve thousand mounted archers: The Greek text reads 120,000, as noted above, where there appears to be a scribal error in the number, and the cipher 0 should be deleted so that 12,000 remains, as in our text. For who has ever led so many thousands of cavalry into battle? Indeed, Darius led against Alexander only 45,000 cavalry and 200,000 infantry, according to Curtius, Book 4 — unless one should say that the mounted archers here were 12,000, while the remaining cavalry armed with lances, sarissas, battle-axes, etc., numbered 108,000, so that both together would make 120,000. Perhaps in the Greek, with the hundred removed, one should read 20,000, of which 12,000 were archers as the Greek also has, and the rest of another type of armament.

He came to the great mountains of Ange, which are on the left side of Cilicia: Ange therefore was then the name of the mountains which were on the left of Cilicia, from which originated Philtes the Angean, mentioned by Curtius, Book 3. In Herodotus, Book 7, a great mountain called Rangaeus is named, and likewise in other authors.

He stormed and plundered the most famous city of Melothi and all the sons of Tharsis: Melothi in Hebrew signifies fullness, namely of riches and people, such indeed was Melothi. These sons of Tharsis are those spoken of in 2 Maccabees 4:30: "It happened that the Tarsians and Mallotans raised a sedition because they had been given as a gift to Antiochis, the king's concubine." For Tharsis was a grandson of Japheth (Gen. 10), from whom the Mediterranean Sea and Cilicia its boundary, and thence any sea whatsoever, was called Tharsis, as I noted at Isaiah 2:16.
Mambre: The Greek texts read Arbonai.
Even to the borders of Japheth: This signifies Joppa, says Serarius, which is also called Japha today, and by the Turks Zapha; for as Pliny says, Book 5...
The Greek word used here is "ekyklōsen," meaning "he surrounded" or "he encircled," indicating a complete military encirclement.

The Greek text differs from the Latin Vulgate, reading: "He consumed the flocks and herds, plundered the cities, laid waste the fields, and struck down all their young men with the edge of the sword."

Lapide comments on the cutting down of sacred groves (lucos), which were used in the manner of the Gentiles to worship gods and idols, where reptiles performed their obscenities, as stated in the book of Kings.

Lapide remarks on the pride of a man who, forgetting that he is earth and dust, declares war on God and divinity, wishing to be regarded and worshipped as God — indeed as the only God. This occurred because he had conquered Aphaxad. He cites Darius in Curtius (lib. 4): "Human frailty is excessive forgetfulness in prosperity." He compares this state to that of Lucifer (Isaiah 14:15), Pharaoh (Ezekiel 29:3), and the King of Tyre (Ezekiel 28), all of whom deserved to be laid low and stripped of kingdom and life. Pride therefore is pure madness, which makes a man not merely a Titan fighting giants, but a theomachos — one who wars against God himself. # GEM

Tremor and horror invaded their senses. The Greek text reads more fully: "And they feared greatly before him, and were troubled for Jerusalem and the temple of the Lord their God, because they had recently come up from captivity, and all the people had lately been gathered from Judea, and the vessels, the altar, and the house of God (i.e., the temple) had been sanctified after profanation." Many scholars, as noted in the prolegomena, following Bellarmine, Salian, and others, refer these events to Manasseh king of Judah, who, having been captured and taken to Babylon, was released and returned to his kingdom, and in repentance purified the temple he had profaned with idols, restoring true worship of God. But this was a captivity and liberation of Manasseh the king alone, not of the whole people. Moreover, Manasseh the king is nowhere mentioned in this entire book, though he certainly should have been, since it pertained to him as king — rather than to Eliachim the high priest — to fortify Judea against Holofernes and to garrison the mountain passes. Hence others more correctly refer these events to the time of Xerxes, when the temple, profaned and burned by the Chaldeans, had already been rebuilt.

One may ask: How could the Jews, recently returned from captivity, have had such strength and resolve as to dare oppose such numerous and powerful armies? The answer is that this was done out of fear of captivity, whose bitterness and miseries they had recently experienced in Babylon, and out of fear of losing true religion. For Nebuchadnezzar threatened God and the temple with destruction, since he wished to be worshipped alone as God. This seemed intolerable to the Jews who worshipped the true God, so that they preferred to die rather than permit it. Moreover, the Jewish population was innumerable and multiplied by generation like frogs and fishes, as in the time of Moses (Exodus 1). For Josephus, in Antiquities Book 11, chapter 4, counts four thousand thousands plus 620,000 adult males who returned from the Babylonian captivity in the second year of Darius Hystaspes, from the tribes of Judah and Benjamin alone, to whom very many from the other ten tribes attached themselves. Thus they could easily assemble a large army to oppose Holofernes, especially in the mountain passes and precipices, where one man can resist ten enemies.
And they gathered grain. For the fields had recently been harvested, as the Greek adds.
Priest Eliachim wrote to all those who were against Esdraelon, which faces the great plain near Dothaim, and to all through whose territory there could be passage.
That they should hold the ascents of the mountains through which there could be a way to Jerusalem, and guard there where the narrow path could be between the mountains.
All the people cried out to the Lord with great urgency, and humbled their souls in fastings and prayers, both themselves and their wives.
The priests clothed themselves in sackcloth, and the infants were prostrated before the face of the temple of the Lord, and they covered the altar of the Lord with sackcloth.

They cried out to the Lord God of Israel with one accord, that their children might not be given as prey, nor their wives as spoil, nor their cities destroyed, nor their holy things polluted, and that they might not become a reproach to the nations.
Then Eliachim, the great high priest of the Lord, went about all Israel and addressed them.
Saying: Know that the Lord will hear your prayers, if you continue persevering in fastings and prayers in the sight of the Lord.

Be mindful of Moses the servant of the Lord, who cast down Amalek — who trusted in his own strength, his power, his army, his shields, his chariots, and his horsemen — not by fighting with the sword, but by praying with holy prayers.
So shall it be with all the enemies of Israel, if you persevere in this work which you have begun.
So that even those who offered holocausts to the Lord offered sacrifices to the Lord girded with sackcloth, and there was ashes upon their heads.
And with their whole heart all prayed to God, that He would visit His people Israel.

On "all the people cried to the Lord with great urgency": Note here the practice, manner, and power of public prayer and penance in public calamity, to be imitated by Christians so that they may similarly avert their own calamities. Five things were done: first, all the people cried out with great urgency; second, they humbled their souls in fastings and prayers; third, they clothed themselves in sackcloth; fourth, they prostrated the infants before the Lord and the temple; fifth, they covered the altar with sackcloth — so that by the public mourning of religion, justice, temple, and altar, they might provoke God to pity and aid, and, as Tertullian says in the Apology, compel Him as if by a crowd assembled and force applied. The Ninevites did the same (Jonah 2) and the Maccabees (1 Macc. 3:14), and therefore all merited to be heard and delivered by God. The Greek text has more fully: "The people were fasting and praying for many days throughout all Judea and Jerusalem before the face of the Holy Place" — that is, the Temple. Those in Jerusalem went to the Temple to pray; those outside turned their faces toward the Temple.

Then Eliachim, the great priest of the Lord, went about all Israel — in order to exhort all to constancy, confidence, and perseverance in prayers and fasts, promising them victory if they did so. St. Ambrose (Sermon 86) says: "The precepts of Christ are weapons for Christians, and divine fear drives away from us the terror of the enemy. Our weapons, with which the Savior has equipped us, are prayer, mercy, and fasting. For fasting protects better than a wall; mercy liberates more readily than plunder; prayer wounds from farther away than an arrow. For an arrow strikes only a nearby visible adversary, but prayer wounds an enemy placed far away." The Greek text adds more fully: Joachim the High Priest and all the priests who stood before the Lord and those who ministered to the Lord, girded with sackcloth about their loins, offered the continual burnt offering and vows and the voluntary gifts of the people; and dust was upon their tiaras or mitres, and they cried to the Lord with all their strength for good — that is, that He might visit all the house of Israel to do good and bring help. Let bishops and princes imitate this High Priest: let them go about, visit, encourage, instruct, and correct the peoples entrusted to them. # GEM

Be mindful of Moses the servant of the Lord, who defeated Amalek, etc. — See the history in Exodus 17, where I have explained it. Note: Eliachim, just as Moses, here prescribes two things for battle: first, that they invoke God and seek and expect victory from Him; second, that they fortify their cities, occupy the passes, and gather all human resources for resistance. For God does not help the idle, but those who labor and fight. The pagan Cato in Sallust's Catilinarian War saw this, saying: "Not by vows nor by womanly supplications are the aids of the gods obtained; by watching, acting, and taking good counsel, all things turn out prosperously." And indeed: "The Thunderer on high helps not the one who thunders but the one who strives."

On "the children of Israel prepared themselves to resist": The Greek adds that they placed obstacles in the fields — that is, barriers such as hidden trenches, walls, and sharp stakes, into which the Assyrians passing carelessly would fall or stumble.

On "he said to them: Tell me who this people is": From this it is again gathered that these events occurred not under Manasseh but under Xerxes. For under Manasseh, the Babylonians and Assyrians had learned well enough from Manasseh and the captive Jewish leaders who and what the people of the Jews were. Hence those who wish these events to have occurred under Manasseh say that Holofernes' question was not one of ignorance, but of wonder and indignation, contemptuously dismissing the Jews as worthless.
On "and why, above all who dwell in the East": The Greek reads "in the West" rather than "in the East."

On "Then Achior, the leader of all the children of Ammon": Serarius understands the Idumaeans to be included among the children of Ammon. For since they were brothers of the Jews (Jacob, the father of the Jews, was brother to Esau, or Edom, the father of the Idumaeans), when they wished to attack the Jews, they called themselves Ammonites, not Idumaeans, lest it be openly held against them that they were attacking their own brothers. Hence in 2 Paralipomenon 20:1 it is said: "There gathered together the Moabites, and the children of Ammon, and with them others from the Ammonites against Jehoshaphat." There you see the Ammonites distinguished from the children of Ammon. Regarding which the book of traditions states: By Ammonites the Idumaeans are to be understood, who out of reverence for their paternal name were unwilling to take up arms against Israel in their customary guise, but transformed themselves into the appearance of Ammonites.
On "this people is of the race of the Chaldeans": Because Abraham, the father of the Hebrews, was called by God out of Chaldea.

On "the face of thy saints": The Greek reads "sanctified ones," that is, thy faithful who have dedicated themselves to thee through circumcision, and now again through fasting, prayer, and suppliant petitions wholly sanctify themselves to thee, that is, dedicate and consecrate themselves.

On "for thou dost not forsake those who presume upon thee": that is, those who trust in thee; for these above all others and before all else take God as the leader and commander of their works and wars, and through him hope for certain victory. Thus St. Germanus, in order to escape a raging fire, would not allow himself to be moved by others, acting on the presumption of faith.

Lapide comments on those who "presume upon themselves" (de se praesumentes), that is, those who rely on themselves and their own powers. He explains this by adding "and those who glory in their own virtue, He humbles." He cites St. Bernard, *De Gradibus Humilitatis*, grade 7: "He who thinks himself to excel others, how does he not presume more of himself than of others? He sits first in assemblies, he answers first in councils, he approaches uninvited, he intrudes himself unsent, he reorders what has already been ordered." # GEM

They consoled him, but did not admit him to their counsels or place him in command of the war, because the loyalty of defectors is suspect. Lapide illustrates this with examples: Judith feigning defection to the Assyrians, Chusai going to Absalom (2 Kings 17), Sinon going to the Trojans, and Zopyrus (general of Darius Hystaspes) going to the Babylonians — all of whom betrayed those who received them to their enemies.

After the frugal meal, all the people were assembled and prayed through the entire night within the church (synagogue), seeking help from the God of Israel. Lapide cites St. Jerome (Epistle to Eustochium): "You must always eat so that prayer and reading follow food," and (to Laeta, on the education of a daughter): "Eat so that you are always hungry, so that immediately after food you can read and sing psalms." He also cites Tertullian (Apology 39), who refutes the pagan slander about Christian gluttony and describes the Agape feast: prayer precedes the meal; one eats only as much as the hungry desire; one drinks only what is fitting for the chaste; all are satisfied remembering they must worship God through the night; they converse knowing the Lord hears; after washing of hands and lighting of lamps, each is invited to sing to God from Scripture or from his own gift; then prayer closes the banquet, and all depart not into brawling gangs or roaming crowds or outbursts of licentiousness, but with the same care for modesty as those who have dined not so much on a meal as on discipline. Lapide urges the faithful to read this and imitate the frugality and piety of the early Christians at their meals. He notes that "within the church" means within the synagogue — a place of assembly and prayer, called in Greek προσευχή — since the Jews had no temple in Bethulia but only in Jerusalem, and in each major city had a synagogue in place of a temple. He also notes here the antiquity of nocturnal vigils, in which on great feasts or pressing calamities the whole people kept watch in the temple at Jerusalem or, outside Jerusalem, in the proseucha (synagogue), citing Josephus (Life): "The next day the people assembled in the proseuchas, which they call the large house of prayer," and Philo (Life of Moses, bk. 3): "Among the Jews there are places in the towns set apart for sacred prayers, which the common people call proseuchas." For these sacred vigils see also St. Basil on Psalm 114, Eusebius (Ecclesiastical History 6, canon 7), and Tertullian (To His Wife 2, ch. 4). # GEM
Holofernes on the next day commands his armies to march against Bethulia, marshaling his vast forces for the siege.

The infantry numbered 120,000 fighting men (the Greek text gives 170,000), with 22,000 cavalry, besides auxiliary forces drawn from captive peoples and the youth of all the provinces and cities that had been subjugated.

All prepared together for battle against the children of Israel, advancing along the mountain ridge from a place called Belma to Chelmón, which lies opposite Esdrelon, covering the heights overlooking Dothan.

When the children of Israel saw the multitude of enemies, they prostrated themselves on the ground, cast ashes upon their heads, and with one accord prayed that the God of Israel would show His mercy upon His people — a notable act of corporate humiliation and prayer before God.

Taking up their weapons, the Israelites stationed themselves at the narrow mountain passes, guarding these strategic points day and night, recognizing that the terrain was their chief natural defense.

Holofernes, reconnoitering the area, discovered that the aqueduct supplying Bethulia ran from the south outside the city walls, and he immediately ordered it to be cut — a strategically decisive move to reduce the city by thirst rather than by assault.
There were, however, springs not far from the walls from which the besieged could secretly draw water, though this was barely sufficient for refreshment rather than for full drinking needs.

The Ammonites and Moabites came to Holofernes counseling him not to rely on lance or arrow against Israel, for the mountains and steep hills defended them naturally; they advised a strategy of cutting off the water supply entirely.

Their counsel was to post guards at all the springs, so that without sword or open battle the Israelites would either perish of thirst or, exhausted, would surrender their city — which they believed could not be taken by force owing to its mountain position.
This counsel pleased Holofernes and his officers, and he stationed centurions around every spring in a complete cordon, encircling the city's water sources.

After twenty days of this siege and blockade, the cisterns and water reserves of Bethulia were entirely exhausted, so that not enough water remained within the city to satisfy even one day's need, the water being rationed daily by measure to the people.
Then all the men, women, young people, and children of Bethulia gathered together before Ozias with one voice in a single assembly to demand resolution of their crisis.

They cried out accusing Ozias: "Let God judge between us and you, for you have brought evil upon us by refusing to treat peaceably with the Assyrians, and therefore God has sold us into their hands" — an expression of despair and misplaced blame upon their leader.
They lamented that there was no one to help them as they lay prostrate before the enemy, perishing of thirst and facing great destruction.
They demanded that all the inhabitants be assembled so that the city might voluntarily surrender itself to the forces of Holofernes.

They argued: "It is better for us as living captives to bless the Lord than to die and become a reproach to all flesh, watching our wives and children perish before our eyes" — a reasoning born of desperation rather than true faith.

They called heaven, earth, and the God of their fathers to witness, acknowledging that God was punishing them for their sins, and they urged that the city be handed over to Holofernes' army, preferring a swift death by the sword to the prolonged agony of dying of thirst.
When they had spoken, great weeping and wailing arose throughout the assembly, and for many hours they cried out with one voice to God.

In their prayer they confessed: "We have sinned with our fathers, we have acted unjustly, we have committed iniquity" — a corporate confession of inherited and personal sin, recalling the pattern of penitential prayer found throughout Scripture.

They appealed to God's mercy: "Because You are merciful, have mercy on us, or chastise our iniquities by Your own scourge, but do not hand over the people who confess You to a people that does not know You."
They feared above all the blasphemy of the Gentiles, who would mock saying: "Where is their God?" — a concern for the honor of God's name that elevates their prayer beyond mere self-interest. # GEM
When wearied by these cries and exhausted by weeping they fell silent, Ozias rose in tears to address the assembly.

Ozias urged them to be of good courage: "Be of equal mind, brothers, and let us wait these five days for the mercy of the Lord" — counseling patient endurance and trust rather than desperate surrender.
He expressed hope that God might yet cut short His wrath and give glory to His own name, trusting that divine honor would move God to act on behalf of His people.
He conceded that if after five days no help came, they would do as the people had proposed — a compromise reflecting Ozias' pastoral prudence in neither crushing hope nor abandoning realism.

Commentary on "from the place called Belma to Chelmon": Belma is a town at the foot of the mountain on which Bethulia is situated, called Abellina by Brocardus. Near Dothan is the city of Chelmon, or as the Greek text has it, Cyamon. Holofernes pitched camp in both locations, as the Greek text states, and drew up his battle line for the assault and siege of the city.

Commentary on "that the spring which flowed might be directed through the aqueduct": that is, so that the water might be conveyed into the city through the aqueduct. Accordingly, Holofernes cut the aqueduct and diverted the water away from the city. Serarius conjectures that for "dirigeret" one should read "dirigaret," meaning "would irrigate"; but "dirigeret" suits this passage well enough, for a spring directs and transmits water into the city through a channel.
Commentary on "The sons of Ammon and Moab came to Holofernes": The Greek text reads: "the princes of Idumaea and Moab," for the Idumaeans, attacking the Jews, pretended...

[On JUDITH THE WIDOW] Some hold that "Judith" is a common name, just as "Abra" is, but here appropriated to this heroine. For the Hebrew "Yehudi" means the same as "Jewish," and so "Yekudit," and by crasis "Judith," means the same as "Judaea."

More correctly, Judith is a proper name whose etymology aptly fits here. For "Judith" in Hebrew means the same as "confessing, praising, glorifying" — namely, God; for the Hebrew "hoda" signifies to confess, to praise, to celebrate. For this purpose Judith withdrew from the crowd into her own oratory, to pray and praise God continually.

Hear St. Fulgentius, Epistle 2, on the widowed state, citing these words about Judith: "Behold a widow illustrious by birth, rich in resources, young in age, lovable in beauty — she despised riches, rejected delights, trampled upon the incitements of the flesh, and clothed in virtue sought not to serve a second marriage." Therefore by the testimony of so distinguished a work it appeared how greatly widowed continence is beloved by God.

[On DAUGHTER OF MERARI, SON OF IDOX] Note: Only sixteen generations and the same number of Judith's ancestors are enumerated here, although from Jacob, the common patriarch of the Jews, up to the Babylonian captivity — which preceded Judith's era — Matthew 1 counts 26 generations. Therefore one of two things must be said here: either not all of Judith's ancestors are enumerated, just as Eliezer's are not fully enumerated either.
[On the rebuke of Uzziah] Judith rebukes the elders, saying: "Who are you to test the Lord?" This is not a word that provokes mercy, but rather one that stirs wrath and kindles fury. # GEM

[On setting a time for God's mercy] You have set a time for the Lord's mercy and appointed a day for Him according to your own judgment — a presumptuous act by which men arrogantly impose limits upon divine providence.

[On patience and repentance] But because the Lord is patient, let us in this very situation repent and seek His indulgence with tears poured out; for God will not threaten as a man does, nor be inflamed to anger as the son of man.

[On humiliation of soul] Therefore let us humble our souls before Him, and serving Him with a humbled and contrite spirit, let us speak weeping to the Lord, that He may deal with us according to His will and His mercy.

[On the faithfulness of Israel] We have not followed the sins of our fathers who forsook their God and worshipped strange gods, for which crime they were given over to the sword, to plunder, and to confusion before their enemies. We know no other God but Him.

[On awaiting God's consolation] Let us await His consolation with humility, and He will require our blood from the afflictions of our enemies, and will humble all the nations that rise up against us, and will make them without honor — the Lord our God.

[On Abraham tested] They ought to remember how our father Abraham was tested and proved through many tribulations, and was made the friend of God — a model of how tribulation is the path to divine friendship. # GEM

[On the pattern of the saints] So likewise Isaac, Jacob, Moses, and all who pleased God passed through many tribulations faithfully. This is the universal pattern of the saints — fidelity proven through suffering.

[On those who could not bear trials] But those who did not receive temptations with the fear of the Lord, and brought forth their impatience and the reproach of their murmuring against the Lord, were destroyed by the destroyer and perished by serpents — a reference to the punishment of the Israelites in the desert.

[On the purpose of chastisement] We should believe that these very punishments are lesser than our sins deserve, and that the scourges of the Lord, by which we are corrected as servants, have come for our amendment and not for our destruction.

[On Judith's holiness] Uzziah and the elders say to her: "Everything you have said is true and there is no fault in your words. Now therefore pray for us, because you are a holy woman and one who fears God."

[On Judith's plan being from God] Judith replies: Just as what I was able to say you recognize as being of God, so also prove whether what I have resolved to do is from God, and pray that God may make my counsel firm.

[On secrecy of action] I do not want you to investigate my action; until I report back to you, let nothing else be done except prayer for me to the Lord our God — showing that Judith's plan required both divine assistance and human intercession.
Lapide notes that David is counted among the ancestors, reckoning 28 generations and forebears, the sentence being incomplete in the source.

Lapide notes that while some codices read differently, the corrected Roman codices consistently have "filii Ruben" — but this Ruben is not the Patriarch and firstborn of Jacob, but rather a distinguished man from the tribe of Simeon, through whom Judith's lineage is traced and at whom the genealogy terminates, not proceeding further to Simeon the Patriarch, Jacob's second son. This is confirmed by the fact that Reuben the firstborn of Jacob had no son named Simeon, as is clear from Genesis 46:9 and 1 Chronicles 5:3. So Lyra.

On "her husband Manasseh": the Greek adds that he was from the same tribe and homeland as Judith. On "he oversaw" (instaurat): the Greek has epestē, meaning "he stood over," leading some to think Manasseh was superintendent not of a private but of a public harvest, i.e., overseer of the whole plain of Bethulia — just as at Rome there were public overseers of grain and provisions. Josephus in his Life likewise mentions that the superintendency of the great plain was entrusted to Arbutio.
On "six months": the Greek reads "four months."

On Judith making for herself a private chamber in the upper story of her house (i.e., in the loft or solarium) and dwelling there shut in with her maidservants: this was done both to flee men and the crowds of visitors, thereby guarding her chastity and reputation, and to devote herself wholly to prayer and God. This therefore is the first mark of Judith's widowhood: love of silence and solitude. Hence St. Jerome, in his Letter to Furia the widow, gives this counsel: "Flee the company of young men; do not go out in public, carried about with the freedom of widows behind a retinue of eunuchs. Seek the society of holy virgins and widows. And if necessity compels you to speak with men, do not shun witnesses, and let your confidence in conversation be such that when another enters, you neither tremble nor blush. The face is the mirror of the mind, and silent eyes confess the secrets of the heart." And further: "Redeem virgins to bring into the King's chamber; receive widows whom you may mingle among the lilies of virgins and the roses of martyrs as certain violets; and in place of the crown of thorns in which Christ bore the sins of the world, weave such garlands."

On "wearing a hairshirt upon her loins": the hairshirt continually pricked and stung the flesh with its bristles like needles, and by that pain suppressed and extinguished the impulses of shameful pleasure boiling up with young blood. For the hairshirt, being constant and continuous, subdues these impulses more effectively than the passing pains of flogging. Therefore young people, especially widowed women, should arm themselves with a hairshirt as a most powerful defense for subduing the flesh and preserving chastity. See our Gretser, On Disciplines. For as St. Jerome says, Epistle 11 to Ageruchia the widow: "It is certainly much more laborious not to enjoy what you have than to desire what you have lost. Hence virginity is easier in that it does not know the incitements of the flesh; while widowhood is more anxious in that the mind recalls past pleasures." And before this, citing examples of chastity from pagan matrons who preferred chastity to life, he says: "I shall briefly mention the queen of Carthage, who chose rather to burn..."  # GEM

[Note: This text is actually commentary on Judith 8, not 2 Chronicles 8.] On Judith's perpetual fasting except on Sabbaths: The Jews fasted from morning until evening, taking no food before the appearance of the evening star (Hesperus) and the stars — as the Turks still fast to this day.

Fasting is described as the third dowry of Judith, namely perpetual fasting, which withdrew the fuel of luxurious delicacies — indeed even necessary foods — from the fire of lust. Note the maxim: "Withdraw the wood from the fire, if you wish to extinguish the flame."

St. Jerome (Epistle 9, to Salvianus, on preserving widowhood) is cited at length: "You who have buried all pleasures alike in your husband's tomb, who washed your face with tears over his bier rather than with rouge and white lead, who took up a dark tunic and black shoes in place of bright clothing and gilded slippers — you need nothing else but to persevere in fasting. Let pallor and austerity be your jewels; let the softness of feathers not warm your youthful limbs; let the heat of baths not inflame the fresh blood of a young woman." # GEM

Jerome further cites a pagan poet speaking in the person of a continent widow: "He who first bound me to himself took away my love; let him keep it with him and preserve it in the tomb." Jerome then argues: if all pagan widows condemn pleasures by the common law of nature, how much more is expected of a Christian widow, who owes her chastity not only to her deceased husband but to Him with whom she shall reign?

Jerome continues (Epistle 9): "It is far better for the stomach to ache than the mind; to rule the body than to serve it; to stagger in step than in chastity." And (Epistle 10, to the widow Furia): "The burning arrows of the devil must be turned back by the rigor of fasts and vigils; for a widow who lives in luxury is living dead (1 Tim. 5)." # GEM

Jerome adds: "The Apostle mortifies his own body and subjects it to the dominion of the soul, lest he fail to practice what he preaches — and shall a young woman with a body inflamed by food feel secure about her chastity?" He clarifies he does not condemn foods which God created for use with thanksgiving, but removes incitements to pleasure for the young. He declares that youthful bones filled with wine and rich foods burn with greater ardor than Etna, Vulcan's isle, Vesuvius, or Olympus. # GEM

Jerome advises abstinence from wine and meat, recommending a diet of vegetables for young Christians. He adds: "Whatever fosters the seedbed of pleasures, regard as poison. Sparse food and a stomach always somewhat hungry is preferable to three-day fasts. It is much better to eat a little each day than rarely to eat one's fill. The best rain is that which descends gently upon the earth; a sudden and excessive downpour sweeps the fields away headlong. When you eat, remember that you must pray and read immediately afterward." # GEM

On "her husband had left her great riches": Judith did not use these for herself but distributed them for the use of the poor. St. Bernard (Sermon on the Feast of All Saints) is cited: "What is more admirable, or what martyrdom heavier, than to hunger amid feasts, to shiver amid many precious garments, to be oppressed by poverty amid the riches which the world offers, which the evil one displays, which our own appetite desires? Shall not he rightly be crowned who strives thus — rejecting a promising world, mocking a tempting enemy, and (what is most glorious) triumphing over himself and crucifying his itching concupiscence?" # GEM

On "she was exceedingly beautiful" — interpreted as meaning of most approved reputation, or most celebrated for the fame of her probity and holiness — "because she feared the Lord greatly": This fear was not servile but filial, by which Judith worshipped and revered God as Lord, indeed as her Father, in the highest degree. She therefore strove to please Him in all things and took care not to offend Him in any matter, however small. By this holiness she prepared herself for so great a deed, so that she might be worthy to be chosen by God to overturn the enemy's camp and to liberate the people. # GEM

On "Who are you that tempt the Lord?" (Judith 8:11): 'Tempt' means to embitter, provoke, and stir to anger, while prescribing laws to Him that unless He aids you within five days you will surrender the city to the enemy — when rather you ought to invoke Him with humble prayer that He have mercy on you and Himself appoint for you the day and manner of your deliverance.

On "For God will not threaten as man threatens" (Judith 8:15): The meaning is: let us not fear the threats of Holofernes but those of God; for Holofernes' threats are arrogant, empty, and vain, as are those of other men; but God's threats are certain, fixed, and efficacious. For God when angered surely and inevitably inflicts what He threatens.
On "Let us humble our souls before Him" (Judith 8:16): Through humble penitence, fasting, prayer, and supplication, that He may forgive us our sins and deliver us from Holofernes.
On "With your speech lift up their hearts" (Judith 8:21): That is, lift them to magnanimity and firm confidence in God as their deliverer.

On "Abraham was tested and through many tribulations was made a friend of God" (Judith 8:22): Especially when commanded by God to sacrifice his only-begotten son Isaac he was willing to sacrifice him (Gen. 22). See what is said there. # GEM

On "All who pleased God passed through many tribulations faithfully" (Judith 8:23): That is, faithfully cleaving to God, seeking and awaiting deliverance from tribulation from Him. See the commentary on James 1:1 and following, and Ecclesiasticus 1:1 and following.

On "Let us not therefore take vengeance for ourselves" (Judith 8:26): That is, let us not in the manner of those seeking revenge be indignant and murmur against God, as if wishing to take vengeance on Him for the calamities and fears He sends upon us.

On Judith's words "As what I was able to speak, you recognize to be of God" (Judith 8:30): That is, suggested and inspired to her by God — so she attributes her wisdom entirely to divine inspiration, not to herself.
Remember, O Lord, Thy covenant — that is, the covenant made with the Hebrews through Moses, David, and other leaders.
